news.title: Error led to 400 noncitizens voting in New Jersey, governor says
news.text:
Roughly 400 noncitizens voted in New Jersey after about 6,600 residents were improperly registered to vote between June 2023 and June 2024 because of a software error at the Motor Vehicle Commission, Gov. Mikie Sherrill announced today.

All of the people affected had disclosed that they were not citizens when registering for either a driver’s license or ID, but were nonetheless placed on the voter rolls in error.

Those people will be removed from the voter rolls. The governor said that they may now face federal prosecution or unexpected hurdles — “through no fault of their own” — as they work toward becoming citizens.

“We are working very hard to protect them,” Sherrill, a Democrat who took office in January, said at a news conference.

The New York Times could not independently verify the figures disclosed Tuesday.

The software vendor Sherrill said was responsible for the error, Idemia, was being phased out of use in New Jersey, the governor said.

The state had “no evidence, at this time, that any elections were swayed,” she noted, including the 2024 presidential contest.

Officials with Idemia could not be immediately reached for comment.

Officials within the Sherrill administration became aware of the “serious software error” last week, the governor said, and began an immediate investigation into what had happened.

The error was discovered and corrected in 2024, while Phil Murphy was governor, but never publicly disclosed, Sherrill said.

A spokesperson for Murphy, a Democrat who served for two terms, did not reply to emails, calls or a text message.

Sherrill was unstinting in her criticism of the way the Murphy administration handled the problem, citing “reckless failures.”

“It’s unacceptable that the vendor released software with such a glaring error. It’s unacceptable that the MVC took a year to get this issue fixed. And it’s unacceptable that no one in the previous administration brought this to light, demanded accountability or took action when it happened years ago,” Sherrill said.

The finding that roughly 400 noncitizens had cast ballots in New Jersey is one of the larger confirmed instances of noncitizens voting in U.S. elections. But it remains an exceptionally small percentage of the overall vote total.

If those 400 noncitizens had all voted in the state’s local elections in 2023, that would reflect just 0.02% of the electorate. If they voted in the 2024 presidential election, those 400 noncitizens would make up about 0.009% of the state’s overall vote totals.

Kamala Harris, the former vice president who ran against President Donald Trump in 2024, won New Jersey by more than 252,000 votes.

The findings may nonetheless add fuel to claims by Trump and his allies that noncitizens are illegally on voter rolls nationwide and are casting ballots in elections in wide numbers, especially as the president makes a final pitch to Congress to pass the SAVE America Act. The act would limit mail voting and add a documentary proof of citizenship requirement to voting.

“Democrats and their media allies have repeatedly said it is impossible for noncitizens to register to vote, let alone cast a ballot,” a White House spokesperson, Abigail Jackson, said today in an email.

“American voters deserve to have confidence that our elections are safe and secure,” she added.

Joe Gruters, the chair of the Republican National Committee, said that the disclosure confirmed information the party had also learned through a public records request. Gruters, a state senator in Florida, said that it was further evidence of a need for Congress to pass the SAVE America Act.

“Democrats keep claiming this never happens,” Gruters said in a statement, “yet every new discovery tells the same old story. Enough excuses.”

Sherrill contrasted her decision to publicly disclose the error with what she called Trump’s efforts to “weaponize elections for political gain.”

“For the past 10 years, he’s cried wolf and peddled bizarre conspiracy theories about fraud with no evidence,” Sherrill said.

“Trump could care less about the actual integrity of the process,” she added. “His only concern is himself.”

This article originally appeared in The New York Times. 

© 2026 The New York Times Company
news.summary: Roughly 400 noncitizens voted in New Jersey after about 6,600 residents were improperly registered to vote between June 2023 and June 2024 because of a software error at the Motor Vehicle Commission, Gov. Mikie Sherrill announced today.
news.author: New York Times

news.title: President Trump to hold rally in Michigan next week
news.text:
WASHINGTON (AP) — President Donald Trump is set to rally in Michigan next week as early in-person voting begins in the battleground state’s primary, where voters will choose nominees for open Senate and governor’s races.

The rally is set to take place in Oakland County on Monday, according to a person familiar with the plans who was not authorized to discuss the event publicly.

Early in-person voting begins Saturday ahead of the Aug. 4 primary, when voters will also select nominees for U.S. House and other statewide offices. Republican Mike Rogers is unopposed in the Senate primary, while the GOP gubernatorial contest has intensified between Trump-backed U.S. Rep. John James and businessman Perry Johnson.

The visit will allow the president to take his messaging on the road as Republicans grow concerned about how rising gas prices and an unpopular war in Iran might affect their midterm chances. Republicans are defending slim majorities in the House and Senate.

Trump carried Michigan in 2016 and 2024, and both parties view the battleground state as central to the 2026 midterm elections.

The White House did not respond to a request for comment.

Democrats are facing the most competitive race in the Aug. 4 primary, with U.S. Rep. Haley Stevens and progressive candidate Abdul El-Sayed battling for the party’s Senate nomination. The winner will face Rogers, who lost narrowly in 2024 to now-Sen. Elissa Slotkin. 

Republicans have faced a bruising contest for the gubernatorial nomination, however, with Democratic Gov. Gretchen Whitmer term-limited. James has been endorsed by Trump, but Johnson has spent millions attacking James on television and digital advertising.

Johnson’s campaign has already advertised a “Trump Tailgate” on Monday. 

Oakland County, where Trump is set to appear, was long considered one of Michigan’s premier political bellwether counties. Democrats have steadily expanded their margins there even as Trump carried the state in 2016 and again in 2024.

Republicans saw 2026 as a chance to finally gain back power in a state that has been dominated by Democrats — outside of Trump’s wins — over the past decade. But surging gas prices, an unpopular war in Iran and tariffs that have hit the state’s auto industry hard have all contributed to concerns about a worsening political environment for Michigan Republicans.

In May, a Democrat won a special election by almost 20 points in a state Senate district that Kamala Harris won by less than 1 point in 2024. The previous officeholder, a Democrat, carried the seat by 6 percentage points in 2022.
news.author: Joey Cappelletti

news.title: Which direction is Trump’s job approval rating headed?
news.text:
ANALYSIS — The good news for Republicans is that President Donald Trump’s job approval rating isn’t getting any worse. The bad news is that many of the issues that drove his rating down in the first place are making their way back into the news, with the midterm elections creeping closer with each passing day.

After beginning his second term with a 52 percent job approval rating, according to Nate Silver’s national average, Trump embarked on a steady downward slide, slipping to a low of 38 percent near the end of May. Since that low point, the president has seen modest improvement to 40 percent, as of Monday. 

And GOP strategists have been encouraged by some improvement for their party in the generic congressional ballot, which asks respondents whether they plan to vote for the Republican or Democratic candidate for Congress. They specifically point to the latest Washington Post-Ipsos poll, which had Democrats with a 3-point advantage, even though Silver’s average has shown Democrats with a consistent 6-point edge for the past five months. 

Republicans, who are defending narrow majorities on Capitol Hill, are looking for any bits of hope, given the traditional midterm headwinds they face and Trump being in worse shape now compared with his first term. At his lowest point, in mid-2017, Trump was upside down by 20 points on his job approval rating, but he bounced up to being 13 points underwater ahead of his first midterm, when Republicans lost a net of 40 House seats (and netted two Senate seats). 

This time around, Trump is underwater by 22 points, according to G. Elliott Morris' polling average, and is almost six months behind from when he started his 2018 rebound. And even though the president appears to have halted his slide, another downturn looks more likely than a rebound. 

Trump is at risk of squandering the goodwill and enthusiasm from hosting the World Cup and the “America 250” celebration with a series of policy decisions that contributed to his initial political decline.

Despite multiple claims of ceasefires and agreements, the conflict with Iran is back on. Three more U.S. servicemembers have died over the past week, two as a result of a missile attack on an air base in Jordan and one during an Iranian drone detonation at a northern Iraq air base. That has brought the total number of service members killed since the war began in February to 17 as of Tuesday afternoon. And uncertainty over the Strait of Hormuz has pushed the national average price of gas back up to $4 per gallon, according to AAA as of Tuesday.

Two more people were fatally shot by Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents in Texas and Maine, prompting the agency to largely suspend vehicle stops. But Trump promptly reversed that policy reversal. “[We] must be strong, tough, and smart, and we CANNOT give up one of I.C.E.’s most important and effective Crime Fighting tools, THE TRAFFIC STOP!” Trump posted on Truth Social. 

And, unfortunately, more violence related to immigration enforcement appears likely, based on the agency’s track record.

Last week, the president chose to utilize a prime-time address to relitigate his 2020 election loss. Trump’s focus on vote-counting in Georgia has frustrated some Republicans, right from the immediate aftermath of the 2020 election when his fixation contributed to the GOP losing both of Georgia’s Senate seats in January 2021 runoffs. And the president on Monday signed proclamations imposing 50 percent tariffs on certain goods from Canada, a longtime ally that also incurred Trump’s wrath over wildfire smoke drifting from across the northern border.

In total, it almost reads like the greatest hits of Trump’s most unpopular political positions. 

The uncertainty around Trump’s standing comes at a critical moment. Not only are the midterm elections a little more than three months away, but both parties are polling in earnest in dozens of races, calibrating their fall strategies. So even if Republicans are feeling cautiously optimistic about the latest data, that could turn out to be a mirage if the president’s job approval rating takes another turn for the worse. And seats that look solid for the GOP today may not be in a month or two from now. 

But Republicans aren’t waiting on Trump to get dramatically more popular. Rather than the election being a referendum on the president, they’re intent on it framing November as a choice between the two parties. More specifically, Speaker Mike Johnson described it as a “contrast between common sense and crazy” at a recent campaign event for Omaha City Council Member Brinker Harding, who’s trying to succeed Republican Don Bacon in Nebraska’s battleground 2nd District. 

It’s a good strategy considering that the national Democratic Party isn’t popular either. Overall, Republicans believe Democrats are making the wrong choices in key primaries around the country. 

Some of the minority party’s highest-profile primary winners have been affiliated with the Democratic Socialists of America. Even though the seats they won aren’t the most competitive, Republicans plan to portray them as the de facto leaders of the Democratic Party. 

Back in 2018, Republicans held onto Nebraska’s 2nd District, 51 percent to 49 percent, in the face of a Democratic wave after the more progressive candidate won the Democratic primary. But Bacon, a retired Air Force brigadier general, was an established incumbent that cycle, while Harding is trying to win an open seat. The district has also become more Democratic over the past decade. Trump would have finished ahead of Hillary Clinton by 2 points in the 2016 election, under the district’s current lines, while Kamala Harris finished ahead of Trump by 5 points in 2024. 

In the end, the outcome of the midterm elections likely comes back to the president’s standing and how voters feel about the direction of the country. And while it looked just a few weeks ago like Trump and the Republicans were poised for a modest bump, the president who can’t seem to get out of his own way could be headed for another downward turn once again.
news.author: Nathan L. Gonzales

news.title: Louisiana’s Cleo Fields forgoes reelection after redistricting eliminated his seat
news.text:
Louisiana Democrat Cleo Fields said Tuesday that he would not seek reelection this fall after his majority-Black district was redrawn to favor Republicans earlier this year. 

Instead, Fields said he would seek a return to the state Senate, running for his old seat that’s newly open following the recent death of his successor.

“After a great deal of prayer and reflection, I have decided not to seek re-election to Congress, in the 6th District or any other district in Louisiana,” he said in a statement. “I feel my calling to serve the people is better put to use back home than here in D.C., and I intend to answer that call.”

Fields returned to Congress last year nearly three decades after he served two terms in the mid-1990s before retiring after court-ordered redistricting. After leaving Congress, he served two separate stints in the state Senate before a new congressional map was approved in 2024, clearing the way for his return to the House from a redrawn district that stretched from Baton Rouge to Shreveport. 

But in April, the Supreme Court struck down that map, which had given Democrats two majority-Black House seats, finding that it was an unconstitutional racial gerrymander. The state Legislature then adopted new congressional lines, while also delaying the primaries for House races until November. All candidates, regardless of party, will compete on the same ballot, with the two top finishers advancing to Dec. 12 runoffs if no one wins an outright majority. 

Under the new map, the 6th District currently held by Fields, which Kamala Harris carried by 15 points, is essentially dismantled. Trump would have won the new 6th District — a majority-white seat in the Baton Rouge area — by 32 points, according to calculations by Inside Elections with Nathan L. Gonzales. 

Fields’ announcement comes a day after the National Republican Congressional Committee added Louisiana’s 6th District to its list of targeted House seats this fall.

“This fall, Louisianans in the Sixth District will elect a strong, commonsense conservative who will give them the representation they deserve,” NRCC spokesman Reilly Richardson said in a statement.
news.author: Mary Ellen McIntire

news.title: Laura Loomer Apologizes For Downplaying Ukraine War: 'I Feel Like Such An A**hole'
news.text:
Laura Loomer just did something rare for a MAGA lover: admit she was wrong.

The right-wing personality, conspiracy theorist and noted Donald Trump ally is in Ukraine to witness the war with Russia after she last month said on her podcast that she might have been a victim of Russian propaganda. She previously argued against U.S. funding for Ukraine after the Russian invasion and called Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy a “Jihadi apologist.”

She posted Tuesday on X that experiencing an air raid in the war-torn country shifted her opinions on the conflict.

Loomer said after hearing “sirens blaring” and realizing “this is everyday for every Ukrainian,” she now feels “like such an asshole for minimizing the struggle of Ukrainians for the last 5 years.”

“I often said I don’t care. Looking back, that wasn’t very nice of me to say.There are people in Ukraine who have to run to a bomb shelter everyday and some of us had the audacity to say Russia wasn’t wrong because we believed the lie that Ukraine is a country full of Nazi sympathizers.We have been so propagandized by Russia and we don’t even realize it.”

Loomer then used her moment of clarity to shift things back to her normally bonkers content, claiming “air sirens could very well be the future of America if we don’t start demanding our lawmakers crack down on communism and multipolarity,” and warned, “we won’t have Trump forever and the axis of evil is moving fast.”

HuffPost reached out to the White House for comment on the post, but no one immediately responded.

Zelenskyy thanked Loomer in a response post, saying, “It is really important” she is in his country and “sees things with her own eyes as they are.”

Loomer isn’t known for fact-based commentary. She has spread conspiracy theories about Sept. 11 being an inside job, that former Vice President Kamala Harris is not Black and that Ukrainian militants were in the crowds during the Jan. 6, 2021, riot.
news.author: David Moye

news.title: New Jersey put more than 6,000 non-citizens on voter rolls after a software ‘error’
news.text:
A software error in New Jersey’s Motor Vehicle system led to more than 6,000 noncitizens being registered on the state’s voter rolls, the governor said Tuesday.

Between June 2023 and June 2024, approximately 6,600 people who applied for state IDs were automatically registered as voters despite indicating they were not U.S. citizens, New Jersey Governor Mikie Sherrill said in a news release. 

“These individuals answered ‘no’ when asked on a keypad whether they were a U.S. citizen when applying for drivers' licenses and identification cards, but through no fault of their own, the system registered them anyway,” Sherrill said. 

Of the 6,600 people who were added, fewer than 400 individuals voted, a preliminary analysis indicated. 

New Jersey is now removing residents who were “erroneously added” to voter rolls and replacing the vendor who is responsible for administering New Jersey’s system.

Sherrill emphasized that the error occurred under former Governor Phil Murphy’s administration and that she was “appalled by the reckless failures that allowed this to happen.”

“This failure didn’t occur under my watch, but accountability starts now. I am taking action to prevent anything like it from occurring in the future and make sure those responsible are held accountable,” Sherrill said.

“New Jerseyans should have confidence that every eligible citizen can vote, every lawful vote will be counted, and every reasonable step will be taken to protect the integrity of our elections. That's my responsibility, and it's one I will never stop fighting to uphold,” Sherrill said. 

The announcement comes four days after Department of Homeland Security Secretary Markwayne Mullin accused New Jersey and three other states of having more than 250,000 noncitizens on their voter rolls. 

Mullin demanded New Jersey and the other states’ officials comply with the administration’s “SAVE program,” among other election security enhancements, or face consequences, such as revoked federal funding, fines, penalties or even prison time. 

The new mandate is just one piece of the Trump administration’s efforts to implement new election security policies that would add more regulations around voting. While there is no evidence any U.S. election has been swayed by voter fraud, President Donald Trump often promotes the falsehood because he refused to admit he lost the 2020 election to former President Joe Biden.

Critics have said some of Trump’s desired policies, such as requiring proof of citizenship to vote in federal elections, are a form of voter suppression. 

Sherrill, a Democrat, said the discovery of the noncitizens on voter rolls does not give Trump credibility on election integrity.

“Let me be clear: Donald Trump has zero credibility on the issue of election integrity,” Sherrill said. 

She added: “For more than ten years, he has worked to undermine Americans' faith in our elections by spreading lies about legitimate election results, attempting to overturn a free and fair election, encouraging an attack on our Capitol when he couldn't accept the will of the voters, and defunding election security at the federal level.”

David Becker, the executive director at the Center for Election Innovation & Research Executive Director David Becker emphasized that administrative errors, such as the one in New Jersey, “are very rare and affect a very small fraction of voters.”

Becker said research shows noncitizen voting is extremely rare. 

“In this case, the number of wrongful registrations represents a minuscule fraction, roughly 0.1%, of New Jersey’s total active voter registration records in 2024,” Becker said in a press release. 

“The number of potential ballots cast from those incorrect registrations would represent roughly 0.009% of all ballots cast in New Jersey’s 2024 general election, when Kamala Harris defeated President Trump by more than 250,000 votes. Joe Biden actually defeated Trump by a margin three times as large before this error took place,” Becker added.
news.summary: Fewer than 400 ineligible voters, registered to various political parties, cast ballots across the state during local elections in 2023
news.author: Ariana Baio

news.title: White House admits it used keywords to terminate California research grants
news.text:
Federal agencies that terminated more than a thousand research grants at the University of California last year admitted that they used keywords related to diversity, gender, vaccine hesitancy and COVID-19 to screen for projects that ran afoul of the Trump administration’s priorities. 

It’s an admission that lawyers for the research professors say is proof that the agencies illegally cancelled nearly $2 billion in grants. And that’s a major development in a lawsuit filed by UC researchers against the Trump administration to permanently get their grants back. The researchers’ legal team is now asking the judge in the case to skip a trial and formally declare that the White House repeatedly violated the constitution, including the First Amendment. A court hearing on whether that’ll happen is scheduled for Oct. 20.

The federal agencies made the admission about the keywords in signed stipulations filed in federal court last week in a case called Thakur v. Trump that’s been ongoing since last spring.

The stipulations in the court filing show the agencies “did not terminate any grants based on alleged noncompliance with the terms of the grant” and instead used “general criteria, rather than grant-specific assessment of each award’s compliance, or performance,” the lawyers for the plaintiffs wrote. Lawyers for the UC professors now contend that the admission proves three wide-ranging constitutional violations:

1. By using keyword searches to review hundreds of grants and terminate them en masse rather than reviewing them individually on their merits, the Trump administration targeted research it found politically disfavorable, which is a First Amendment violation.2. In terminating 283 Department of Energy grants from states where a majority voted for Kamala Harris in the 2024 election, and leaving untouched hundreds of grants that went to “red” states, the Trump administration wrongly punished researchers for merely living in states the White House politically opposed.3. And by terminating billions of dollars in grants, and never rerouting that money to other grant opportunities, the Trump administration violated the constitution by not spending money in ways Congress specifically required through laws it passed on how research funds should be used.

CalMatters emailed the U.S. Department of Justice, which acts as the legal defense for the federal agencies, on July 16 to ask whether it agrees that the federal grant-making agency stipulations mean the Trump administration violated the constitutional rights of researchers. A spokesperson for the Justice Department didn’t respond by publication time.

History of lawsuit

The judge in this case, Rita F. Lin, has repeatedly sided with the UC professors and issued several preliminary injunctions forcing the federal agencies to restore the grants while she reviews the full merits of the case. This includes the restoration of hundreds of grants at UCLA that the Trump administration sought to suspend over its allegations that the university tolerated antisemitism and permitted transgender women to compete on women’s sports teams.

Those allegations were also the subject of a demand from the White House for UCLA to pay $1.2 billion or experience an additional cascade of research freezes. However, months before Trump sought that settlement UCLA took steps to address antisemitism on campus, including commissioning a task force to recommend ways to create a more welcoming environment for Jewish students.

Lin, a Biden appointee, faulted the administration for disregarding those efforts by UCLA in a preliminary injunction for a different but related lawsuit. The Trump administration’s justification for terminating the grants did not “mention the remedial steps UCLA had already taken to address the issues described,” Lin wrote.

Lawyers for professors in the Thakur case now want Lin to make a final ruling ordering the grants to be restored, rather than a preliminary one. Even if that were to happen, the federal agencies could decide to appeal Lin’s ruling to a higher court. The stakes “are huge,” lawyers for the professors in the Thakur case wrote. If the White House is “allowed to terminate this funding, the researchers, graduate students, and program staff that relied on the grants to pursue their lives’ work will all suffer significant damage to their reputations and careers.” 

The lawyers warn of lifesaving cures that will never be developed, new understandings of diseases that will go unexplored, and the decline in the country’s global standing as an international juggernaut in publicly funded research.

Using keywords to target disfavored grants

The agencies admitted to using keywords in various ways, according to a CalMatters review of the new court filings.

The Department of Transportation identified six grants for projects worth about $42 million to terminate because they sought “transportation equity,” prioritized “disadvantaged communities,” or focused on “diversifying the transportation workforce” and “equity.” 

The termination of three of those grants led by a UC Davis professor resulted in 77 researchers abandoning 79 in-progress projects. Lawyers for the plaintiffs wrote that the project leaders were also forced to lay off or scramble to replace funding for more than 40 graduate and undergraduate research assistants.

The National Institutes of Health admitted to using an internal search tool to flag UC grants that mentioned “health equity,” “work force diversity,” “structural racism,” and “sexual orientation.” The NIH alone suspended or cancelled more than 1,000 UC grants, including nearly 700 at UCLA, related to vaccines, cancer research and disparities in health outcomes. That figure is newly disclosed in a court filing — last year, UCLA indicated that closer to 500 NIH grants were terminated. The health research agency also declared that it’s possible that DOGE, the short-lived federal office that billionaire Elon Musk led in 2025, used AI to target grants for termination. Lawyers for the plaintiffs wrote to the judge that these admissions show the agencies “identified the viewpoints they wanted to suppress, searched the grants they funded for those that expressed those viewpoints, and terminated those grants on that basis.” That violates the researchers’ First Amendment rights, the lawyers wrote. The Environmental Protection Agency, one of the named agencies in the suit, is in the process of settling with lawyers for the UC professors to avoid further litigation.

A separate federal district judge in Massachusetts last week dealt the Trump administration a blow in its ability to terminate grants and withhold federal funding. The White House relied on a legal phrase in many of its funding cancellations — “no longer effectuates . . . agency priorities.” California and 22 other states sued, arguing that there’s no law or right for the federal government to cancel funding based on new program goals and White House desires after the grants were already awarded. The judge agreed.

This story was originally published by CalMatters and distributed through a partnership with The Associated Press.
news.author: Mikhail Zinshtey/calmatters

news.title: Today in History: July 21, verdict reached in Scopes 'Monkey Trial'
news.text:
In 2012, Turkish-American Erden Eruc became the first person to complete a solo, human-powered circumnavigation of the globe; his journey lasted more than five years.

In 2023, the "Barbenheimer" buzz reached its peak as the films "Barbie" and "Oppenheimer" opened in theaters; the critical and public acclaim for both films led to the fourth-largest weekend box office of all time.

In 2024, President Joe Biden dropped his bid for reelection following a disastrous campaign debate with Donald Trump that raised doubts about the Democratic incumbent's fitness for office. (Trump would go on in the fall to defeat Vice President Kamala Harris, who took Biden's place in the presidential race.)

news.title: Kamala Harris' Stepdaughter Ella Emhoff, 27, Strips Down to a Tiny String Bikini During Beach Outing
news.text:
Kamala Harris' stepdaughter, Ella Emhoff, is continuing to have a relaxing yet productive summer, flaunting her bikini body on the beach while simultaneously making crafts, RadarOnline.com can reveal. 

The 27-year-old model shared a carousel of photos showing off two separate two-piece looks while displaying her design school talents by sewing beads onto a tank top.Crafting on the BeachIn one snapshot, Emhoff sat in a beach lounger in a black-and-white polka-dot bikini from the small-batch, ethically made brand Rat Boi. 

She also included a picture of her crafting kit, which included a bag of colorful beads. Later, she was seen threading a needle and applying them to the white tank. 

Emhoff continued to multitask on the beach in another photo while wearing a racy red string bikini as she appeared to be working on a knitwear swimsuit, displaying the completed number in another picture in the carousel.Ella Emhoff Rocked Multiple Bikini Looks"Me + beach + crafting = perfect summer," Ella captioned the Instagram post. 

Fans took to the comments section with praise for her work. 

"Beading at the beach. That’s the good life," one person wrote, while a second shared, "That beaded shirt is everything."

"So d--- impressed you can do that on the beach and not spill beads everywhere!" a third follower gushed of the Parsons School of Design grad.

"Absolutely adorable and you know how to make us all proud," a fourth fan raved in the comments.Ella Emhoff's Beaded Tank Took '4 Beach Days' to CreateEmhoff brought up the trip in a July 1 post, when she told fans she was getting ready to make a new bikini for her boyfriend Charlie Vessell's family's annual trip to the Sunshine State.

Showing off three of her designs, she admitted she was unhappy with the fit of two of them, with one being too large and the other being too tiny. The gray and blue knit bikini shown in her beach carousel snapshots was one Emhoff eventually decided on. 

In another post, Emhoff showed off the finished product of her tank top, revealing that it took her "four full beach days" while on vacation to sew the intricate beadwork onto the clothing item.Ellla Emhoff and Boyfriend About to Celebrate 3 Years TogetherThe carousel wasn't completely about beach crafting and bikinis. Emhoff shared a loved-up photo of herself kissing Vessell on the forehead. 

The couple will be celebrating three years together at the end of July. As Radar previously reported, Emhoff shared a post in 2025 in a tribute to their second anniversary.

"2 years with Charlie today. I love him so so so so so much. He truly is the best, number 1 guy," the knitwear designer wrote about her artist boyfriend in the social media update. 

Vessell was also by Emhoff's side as she celebrated her 27th birthday in May. She rocked a chic cheetah-print corset top for her big party.
news.summary: Kamala Harris' stepdaughter modeled several bikini looks from her recent Florida vacation, where she crafted on the beach.
news.author: Beth Shilliday

news.title: Maine Democrats are missing a opportunity against Susan Collins
news.text:
On a Friday in late July 2024, I got a call from Joe Biden’s presidential campaign. They wanted to ask me whether I, a New York delegate to the Democratic National Convention, still planned to vote for the then-president for the 2024 Democratic nomination. Roughly 72 hours later, I received an email from New York Gov. Kathy Hochul in my inbox, addressed to New York’s delegates, with the subject line “Uniting Behind Vice President Kamala Harris.” We did. What resulted was a deeply controversial moment in the party’s history — swapping out a nominee through a process that felt democratically legitimate.

Now, two years later, the Maine Democratic Party is facing a similar dynamic following the withdrawal of its Senate nominee, Graham Platner, from a hotly contested and consequential race against Republican incumbent Sen. Susan Collins. It seems the Maine Democratic Party leadership understands it cannot repeat the insider mistakes of the 2024 election, but it still seems to be forgetting another lesson Democrats missed in 2024: crisis breeds opportunity.

While this more open approach is welcome and necessary, simply avoiding past errors is not enough.

Biden paired his decision to drop out of the presidential race with an announcement of full support for Harris as his replacement, even as figures like former President Barack Obama and Rep. Nancy Pelosi reportedly hesitated behind the scenes. As I wrote at the time, a difficult scenario had emerged: Harris was a historic figure, first in line to the presidency, with a resumé better than most others sitting on the not-so-deep Democratic presidential bench, but she would face racism and sexism that some other candidates would not. Yet at the same time, if there was an open process to select Biden’s replacement and voters did not pick Harris, what would that signal to a party base long driven by women and voters of color?

With few good options, and a growing urgency to coalesce around a nominee, my fellow delegates and I voted to nominate Harris. The rest is history: before Biden dropped out, voters had preferred an open convention, and to some, the undemocratic process of swapping in the vice president was alienating.

Now Maine Democrats, faced with a similar scenario seem to have avoided (at least the appearance of) that same closed process. Over the weekend, Democratic voters in the state’s 16 counties chose 500 delegates to join about 100 members of a state party committee at a nominating convention this Saturday in Bangor. Throughout the process, Maine Democrats have emphasized openness. There were multiple candidates, no clear consensus, no Biden-style endorsement to shut down the conversation and even a process for new voices to be selected as delegates since the Platner news broke. 

As of this weekend, supporters of Troy Jackson, former Maine Senate president, seem to have secured most of those open delegate slots. His most prominent rivals have already dropped out of the race, putting him on track to secure the nomination this Saturday in Bangor. 

While this more open approach is welcome and necessary, simply avoiding past errors is not enough. Jackson may have become Platner’s replacement through a process voters view as fairer and distinct from the 2024 effort to swap out Biden, but Democrats seem willing to let the process play out with little fanfare. Jackson will receive the nomination at a convention that is perfunctory rather than spectacular. 

As potential 2028 contender Rahm Emanuel famously noted, “You never want a serious crisis to go to waste.” As few understand better than President Donald Trump, that is particularly true in the modern attention economy.

 A nomination crisis is not just a test of democracy, but an opportunity to showcase it — and win back trust in the process.

All eyes are on Maine, and with the increased stakes, the party should put on a show fit for a leading-edge democracy in 2026. A few weeks was not enough time to totally revamp the procedural processes required. But where were the interactive, nonbinding, statewide polls of Maine Democrats? Where were the citizen-driven candidate forums with top political creators and questions sourced from voters?

And even with the nominee’s identity all but assured, there is still time to get creative and produce a convention that demonstrates agility. According to the Maine Democratic Party website, “only credentialed delegates, credentialed press, and staff will be permitted to attend” the convention, but why not create a lottery system to have voters attend in person? Why not get those voters on the livestream sharing their experience in real time? Invite some nonpolitical local influencers to reach a broader audience. A nomination crisis is not just a test of democracy, but an opportunity to showcase it — and win back trust in the process. Maine Democrats followed an open process that may allow them to skirt criticism, but it matters less if nobody follows along.

Control of the Senate hangs in the balance. Collins has shown a knack for political survival. Avoiding the mistakes of 2024 will not be enough to flip this critical Senate seat. Democrats have a captive audience as the country watches Maine — they should use it to show voters what a modern, messy and robust participatory democracy looks like.
news.author: Ja’han Jones

news.title: Democrats can’t stop Kamala Harris — and it’s got the GOP giddy
news.text:
There are only two lanes to the Democratic presidential nomination two years from now, and one of them is already blocked — by Kamala Harris.

Get ready for the Kamala comeback, followed, in all likelihood, by a replay of her crushing defeat by the Republican nominee.

Believe it or not, the ex-VP occupies what counts as the “center lane” within the party, and for most of the last three decades that’s the one that’s led to the nomination.

Republicans used to be the party of orderly succession, even if the candidate whose turn had come looked like a certain loser: think Bob Dole in 1996.

But recently it’s been Democrats who’ve tended to pick a party elder as their nominee. 

Al Gore, as a sitting veep, might not have been a remarkable choice in 2000, but in ’04 it was John Kerry, in ’16 Hillary Clinton, and in 2020 Joe Biden — with only Barack Obama butting ahead of the line in 2008.

Democrats like a known quantity, a reliable liberal who isn’t too overtly radical.

The radicalism can wait until after Election Day.

So they’re leaning toward running Harris again, even if donors aren’t happy about it: They blew nearly $2 billion on her campaign and aligned PACs in 2024, only for Harris to lose every battleground state to Donald Trump.

It won’t be donors who decide the party’s 2028 nominee, though, and ordinary Democrats actually find Harris reassuring. 

She’s a familiar face, after all, commanding practically universal name recognition.

Harris checks the identity-politics boxes that may be as important as winning to Democratic primary voters: Their ticket must have a woman, and it can’t be all white.

And after being vetted as a VP and undergoing the trial-by-fire of the ’24 campaign, Harris isn’t likely to surprise voters with any extremist tattoos or sexual-assault allegations — things that can’t be taken for granted among Democrats nowadays.

Then there’s the revenge motive: what better way to repudiate the Trump era and reverse the Democrats’ 2024 humiliation than by making the woman he defeated president?

Democratic primary voters are still smarting from Hillary Clinton’s drubbing in 2016.

By 2028, the election of America’s first female president will have been postponed 12 long years, and feminists in the party aren’t willing to wait any longer.

These are the reasons Harris tops almost all polls of 2028 Democratic hopefuls, with an 11.6 point lead in the RealClearPolitics polling aggregate as of mid-July.

“She comes in with 99% name ID and everyone else is going to have to raise money and buy it,” a former Harris aide tells New York magazine’s David Freedlander.

The donors might prefer just about anyone else, yet they’d have to pour an enormous amount of cash into another candidate just to make him or her recognizable.

This has led one longtime Democratic strategist with donors’ ears to welcome the inevitable.

“I hope she runs,” Alex Hoffman told Freedlander, “because then she will lose and we will never have to hear from her again. She will never win an election at a national scale. Never.”

But if Harris is hopeless, why does she seem inevitable?

She has seniority no one else can match, but what about the other lane that leads to the nomination?

That’s the left lane — the far left lane.

The one Bernie Sanders occupied in 2020 and ’16, and that a candidate like Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez might fill in 2028.

Young Dems keep moving leftward, with the Democratic Socialists of America making deeper inroads into the party.

Kamala Harris is not nearly left-wing enough for them — but then, anyone who is would be upsetting to the old guard.

It’s not that the old-timers aren’t also well to the left on economics, but they don’t want to be labeled “socialists” — while the insurgent left is proud of the brand.

The party’s mainstream wants to have it both ways on the Israel-Palestine conflict, while the young left is increasingly explicit about its anti-Zionism.

There are still enough Baby Boomers and suburbanites in the Democratic Party to make the left lane an unlikely road to the nomination.

So Harris can expect a challenge from the left, but the party is still probably one more presidential cycle away from nominating someone who openly represents where Democrats are headed.

In fact, a Harris defeat in 2028 would be the perfect prelude to the socialist and anti-Zionist left’s takeover, discrediting the old guard for forfeiting the election to Trump’s successor.

Democrat insiders know Kamala Harris is a terrible candidate, but the party they built can’t tell her no — even if this is the last nomination they’ll get to make before the DSA takes over.

Daniel McCarthy is the editor of Modern Age: A Conservative Review.
news.summary: Democrat insiders know Kamala Harris is a terrible candidate, but the party they built can’t tell her no.
news.author: Daniel McCarthy

news.title: Maine Democrats are moving on from Platner. But cracks might linger.
news.text:
As Maine Democrats prepare to gather at a hastily scheduled convention in Bangor on Saturday, there’s little suspense around whom the party will tap to replace Graham Platner on the ballot in this must-win Senate race. Former state Senate leader Troy Jackson has garnered so many delegates that his main rivals for the nomination have all ended their campaigns.

But with just over three months to go until November’s vote, it remains unclear whether Democrats can entirely turn the page on the Platner debacle in time to mount a winning campaign against five-term Republican Sen. Susan Collins.

Mr. Jackson, a Platner ally who grew up in a logging family in rural northern Maine, is a progressive Democrat who started his political career as a Republican. Supporters say he can reach voters outside Democrats’ urban base and build a winning coalition in a state that voted for Kamala Harris in 2024 but is far from a Democratic stronghold.

Still, memories of Ms. Harris replacing former President Joe Biden as the presumptive Democratic nominee in July 2024 have underscored the challenges facing eleventh-hour candidates – from scaling up a statewide operation and war chest to forging connections with voters and bridging internal divides. Often, candidates who step in at the last minute are ones who were previously unsuccessful (Mr. Jackson came in third in June’s gubernatorial primary). And Senator Collins has proved an evasive target even in cycles when the environment favored Democrats.

The new nominee “gets the advantage of a fresh start, but they also start without having funds raised and a campaign focused on the Senate,” says Amy Fried, a professor emerita of political science at the University of Maine. Democrats face a well-funded opponent in Senator Collins, a moderate Republican who is the chair of the Senate Appropriations Committee. “I would never bet against Susan Collins winning. She’s been able to do it so many times,” says Professor Fried.

In the town of Ellsworth, a “For Rent” rent sign now hangs above the shuttered Platner campaign headquarters. The former Marine and oyster farmer, once heralded as a rising star on the left, had weathered a string of scandals that drew national attention to this normally under-the-radar state, from a Nazi-linked tattoo to controversial Reddit posts. He quit the race in early July after an ex-girlfriend accused him of sexual assault (which he has denied).

The implosion of Mr. Platner’s campaign roiled Democrats in Maine and beyond, but perhaps nowhere more than here in Ellsworth, where many supporters have personal connections to the candidate or his family. At the Hancock County Democratic caucus over the weekend, where voters gathered at the local elementary and middle school to pick delegates for Saturday’s convention, many talked about the need to move on. But some were still fuming about how Mr. Platner had been abandoned by the party.

Claire Hoyt, a wholesale baker and Platner supporter, had worried that party leaders would try to box out Platner allies. “For the first days after Graham dropped out, it felt like that is exactly what was happening. ... That’s what got everybody activated,” she says.

Now, though, she’s ready to volunteer for the new nominee, working toward the goal of defeating Senator Collins. In the back of her mind is the 2024 presidential nomination, and the various problems that stemmed from divisions within the party. “I’m hoping that we’ve all learned from what happened with Kamala and Biden and that we’re going to do better,” she says.

Other Platner supporters expressed unhappiness at what happened to him.

“We just dropped him; we didn’t give the guy a chance,” says Elaine Gerard, a retiree, who questions why his accuser waited until this month to go public.

Inside the school, Anne Hathaway, a Platner family friend, had put out a poster board on a table for Democrats to write supportive messages to Mr. Platner and his wife. “We still think the world of them,” she says.

Many voters here see Mr. Jackson, who served six years in the state Senate before running for governor this year, as picking up Mr. Platner’s progressive mantle. Two other losing candidates from the gubernatorial race, Secretary of State Shenna Bellows and former Illinois health director Nirav Shah, also briefly sought the Senate nomination but dropped out once it was clear Mr. Jackson was the overwhelming favorite.

While there was little daylight between these candidates on issues such as healthcare funding, progressive taxes, and federal immigration enforcement, the union-backed Mr. Jackson is perhaps the most left-wing. Some observers question whether his platform can win over enough independents and moderate Republicans to win statewide.

Maine voters do split their tickets, says Professor Fried. But ticket-splitters are usually not the white working-class men that Mr. Platner claimed to be reaching; most are middle-class women who vote Democratic but have cast ballots for Ms. Collins as someone they know and like.

Before he dropped out, polls showed that Mr. Platner was lagging behind Ms. Collins among voters without a college degree, Professor Fried notes. “I don’t think he got the support from some of the voters that he wanted to get.”

Mark Longabaugh, a Democratic consultant who works for progressive candidates but was not employed by Mr. Platner, sees the terrain differently. Mr. Platner might have been trailing Ms. Collins among non-college voters, but he was winning overall in most polls. “The question in electoral politics is always a question of margins. So he clearly was doing marginally well enough among working-class voters, with his greater strength among younger, college-educated voters,” he says.

Elections are also decided by turnout, says Mr. Longabaugh, who has worked with Matt Dunlap, the Democratic nominee for Maine’s 2nd Congressional District, a swing district in which Jared Golden, a moderate Democrat, is not seeking reelection. This means exciting the Democratic base, something that progressive candidates right now are doing in Maine and other states. “I really don’t see a lot of sizzle or excitement among moderate, establishment, ‘go along [to] get along’ kind of Democratic candidates at this point,” he says.

That dynamic was apparent in June’s Senate primary, in which Mr. Platner received 156,000 votes in a state with about 1 million voters. His main opponent, Democratic Gov. Janet Mills, who had been drafted to run by Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer, suspended her campaign when it became clear she couldn’t defeat a first-time candidate who was almost half her age.

Maine allows independent voters to participate in party primaries, which analysts say helped Mr. Platner run up the numbers. But only Democrats were eligible to vote for delegates to this weekend’s nominating convention, which officials say was necessary to comply with party bylaws.

Maine’s 24 counties are sending 500 delegates to the convention, alongside 101 state committee members. More than 19,000 Democrats registered to participate in the county meetings, some of which were held virtually.

Back at the Hancock County caucus, Karen Svenson, who works in coastal conservation and knows Mr. Platner’s mother, says she understands Platner supporters’ frustration. But she thinks it’s time to put those emotions aside and focus on winning in November.

“I was very disappointed in Mainers who decided Susan Collins was going to win when Graham Platner fell out. A lot of people threw in the towel,” she says. “I thought, ‘Wait, it’s the movement, not the person. We still have work to do.’”
news.summary: Former state Senate President Troy Jackson is on track to become Maine’s new Democratic Senate nominee this weekend. But eleventh-hour candidacies frequently face big challenges.
news.author: Simon Montlake

news.title: With his nomination all but secured, Troy Jackson begins march to try to defeat Susan Collins
news.text:
This week was supposed to decide Maine’s Democratic Senate nominee. Instead, after former state Sen. Troy Jackson all but cleared the field over the weekend, it has become the unofficial start of the general election against Republican Sen. Susan Collins.

Over a two-day marathon of county meetings, Democratic voters chose the roughly 500 delegates who will select the nominee at a hastily arranged convention this weekend. The vast majority were Jackson supporters, drawn from a slate of delegate candidates his campaign had published last week.

The overwhelming result prompted Jackson’s leading opponents to withdraw by Sunday night, upending a process the Maine Democratic Party had scrambled to build in the three weeks since primary winner Graham Platner dropped out amid scandal.

Political observers in Maine credited Jackson’s organizing power — aided in part by supporters of Platner. Democrats in the state are betting that grassroots enthusiasm can carry Jackson, a logger from the northern part of the state who ran unsuccessfully for governor this year, past Collins in a contest national party leaders see as a prime pickup opportunity in their drive for a Senate majority.

“I think [Jackson’s win] speaks to the organization of the Platner campaign,” said Marcia Myers, chairwoman of the Democratic Party in Hancock County. “They had people together, they identified who they wanted” as delegates.

Andrew Rudalevige, a political science professor at Bowdoin College, told MS NOW that Jackson took advantage of a delegate selection system that allowed the campaigns to identify their hard-core supporters and promote them as potential delegates — even though all delegates remained nominally unpledged.

“There were some folks in the party who said we’ll elect folks who are uncommitted, and there will be a week of deep thought [before the convention],” Rudalevige said. “That was not the stance his campaign took, but that was smart.”

Jackson won’t officially be the nominee until the delegates vote at Saturday’s convention in Bangor, but in Maine and Washington alike, he is already being treated as Collins’ challenger.

His campaign has seen a surge in online fundraising in the last three days through ActBlue, the Democratic Party’s main small-dollar fundraising platform, according to a person familiar with the campaign.

Jackson himself indicated that he has turned his attention to the general election.

“In November, we are going to defeat Susan Collins and reclaim our Senate seat,” he wrote in an X post on Monday.

https://twitter.com/TroyJackson207/status/2079212692590457274

Winning, though, will require unity from Maine Democrats, some of whom told MS NOW in recent days they were “grieving” Platner’s sudden departure from the race.

“There was one thing we had to do when Platner pulled out: keep people together,” said Myers, adding that the rallying around Jackson over the weekend “made me optimistic.”

But not everyone felt the delegate-selection process was a unifying event.

“I think people are going to view it as the party picking the candidate, and that is automatically a weakness,” said a state committee member who was granted anonymity to speak candidly. “We saw that with Kamala Harris in 2024. People perceived it as a coronation instead of people having a fair choice.

“There cannot be any kind of fracture in the party to beat Susan Collins,” the person added.

Jackson, who also ran unsuccessfully for Congress in 2014, will now face a challenge he has never seen in his years in politics, and observers are already noting potential weaknesses.

“I don’t think Jackson has the charisma or ability to speak on his feet that Platner does,” said Rudalevige. “But it comes with a lot less baggage in other areas.”

Jackson is already facing scrutiny. Reports Monday by The Washington Post and CNN described Jackson as a hot-tempered figure during his tenure as state Senate president.

And his evolution on abortion rights — which he opposed earlier in his career before championing pro-choice causes — could stick with some voters.

Nora Walsh-DeVries, vice president of political and legislative affairs at Planned Parenthood Action Fund, told MS NOW previously that she couldn’t speak for voters on whether Jackson’s previous opposition to abortion would be disqualifying for Mainers.

“This has always been about the power building and the seat-flipping over the person, and it will continue to be about that until we get rid of Susan Collins,” she said.

Julianne McShane contributed to this article.
news.author: Steve Benen

news.title: Kalshi blocked from offering contracts in Washington state as judge cites gambling laws
news.text:
A judge has granted Washington’s request to block Kalshi from offering its so-called event contracts in the state, saying the prediction markets platform likely violated the state’s gambling laws.

In a decision on Monday, Judge John McHale of the King County Superior Court granted Washington’s motion for a preliminary injunction.

McHale said the state showed “a likelihood of actual and substantial injury to Washington consumers from illegal gambling activities” if no injunction were issued. He also said the public interest and potential harm to consumers outweighed harm to Kalshi.

Washington joined Massachusetts, Michigan and Nevada in winning court orders restricting Kalshi’s activities.

New York won a similar order on July 8, when a federal judge rejected Kalshi’s bid to block enforcement of that state’s gambling laws against the platform.

The US Commodity Futures Trading Commission, meanwhile, has claimed exclusive jurisdiction over the industry, and challenged regulatory activity in at least nine states.

In April, a divided federal appeals court cited the CFTC’s authority in blocking New Jersey gaming authorities from regulating Kalshi’s sports event contracts.

“States don’t have jurisdiction to regulate prediction markets,” Kalshi said in a statement on Tuesday. “We’re disappointed to see Washington state continue wasting taxpayer dollars.”

The judge said he plans to issue an order implementing the injunction on Aug. 5.

“Kalshi padded their pockets as they promoted illegal betting on sports, elections, the total number of measles cases this year, what will witnesses say during a child trafficking hearing, and even natural disasters,” Washington Attorney General Nick Brown said in a statement. “This victory is the first step toward holding Kalshi accountable.”

Prediction markets such as Kalshi and Polymarket let people wager on the outcome of events such as sports and elections.

They have soared in popularity since the 2024 US presidential election, when they did a better job than pollsters in predicting Republican Donald Trump’s victory over Democrat Kamala Harris.

About $19.04 billion was wagered through Kalshi and Polymarket on the recently completed soccer World Cup, according to data tracker Dune Analytics.
news.summary: Washington joined Massachusetts, Michigan and Nevada in winning court orders restricting Kalshi's activities.
news.author: Reuters

news.title: Ted Cruz Backs Kamala Harris on Election Security and Paper Ballot Proposal
news.text:
Republican Senator Ted Cruz (R-Texas) announced Monday that he agrees with a plan to enhance election integrity promoted by former Democrat vice president and senator Kamala Harris.

“Agreed,” Sen. Cruz wrote on X.com, replying to the following March 31, 2019 post by then-Senator Harris calling for the passage of a bill requiring votes be cast through paper ballots:

“Paper ballots are the smartest, safest way to ensure your vote is secure against attacks by foreign actors. Russia can’t hack a piece of paper like they can a computer. We introduced the Secure Elections Act to ensure our elections are safeguarded. We must be ready.”

As a California senator, Harris co-sponsored Democrats’ “Election Security Act of 2019” to require paper ballots. At the time, she publicly stressed the need for the paper ballot requirement, both on television programs such as ABC’s “The View” and during press events.

Today, however, Harris is aggressively opposing Republicans’ SAVE America Act, which would require voters to provide proof of citizenship and photo identification in order to validate their eligibility to cast ballots.

Such safeguards against voter fraud are tantamount to voter suppression, Harris claimed in a July 16, 2026 X.com post:

“The SAVE Act (sic)is voter suppression. It is part of a larger agenda of conservatives trying to steal power from the people.”

The SAVE America Act passed the Republican-controlled House in February, but has been held up by Senate Democrats’ filibuster ever since. As a result, Republicans have resorted to folding key elements of their effort to ensure that only legal voters vote in U.S. elections into a funding bill, through a process known as reconciliation. Reconciliation requires approval by only a simple majority of the Senate, instead of the two-thirds vote needed to overcome a filibuster.
news.author: Craig Bannister

news.title: Democrat Melat Kiros: U.S. Must Elect More Immigrants from 'Global South' to Challenge Global World Order
news.text:
Socialist Melat Kiros (D), who unseated 15-term Rep. Diana DeGette (D) in Colorado’s first congressional district Democrat primary last month, says the United States must elect more immigrants specifically from the “Global South” to usher in a “reckoning” with the global world order.

“There’s a certain reckoning that we are going to have to have with the global world order as it exists today and the way that it fundamentally will have to shift going forward,” Kiros told a crowd at the Wa’Ela Tigrai conference in Denver, Colorado, whose motto is “Building Little Tigray: Many Cities, One Community.”

“The responsibility that we have as Americans, the recognition that is going to be required of us — of the exploitative and extractive nature that we play on the global scale and the global stage is only going to be challenged if we are getting represented by people who understand that on a fundamental level,” Kiros said. “And that means getting more immigrants into office. That means getting more people who have heritage in the global south into office.”

The “Global South” refers to countries in Africa, Asia, Latin America, and the Caribbean.

Kiros herself is an immigrant from Ethiopia whose father was a beneficiary of the Diversity Visa Lottery in May 1997, just weeks after her birth. The program was created by the Immigration Act of 1990 which was signed into law by then-President George H.W. Bush.

The Diversity Visa Lottery gives out up to 55,000 visas every year to foreign nationals from a multitude of countries, including those with known terrorist problems — such as Afghanistan, Algeria, Egypt, Ethiopia, Iraq, Lebanon, Libya, Nigeria, Saudi Arabia, Somalia, Syria, Trinidad and Tobago, Venezuela, Yemen, and Uzbekistan.

For years, research has consistently shown that the larger a region’s foreign-born population, the more likely that region is to vote for Democrats over Republicans.

Most significantly, in the 2024 election, surveys of foreign-born voters — those are legal immigrants who eventually secured naturalized American citizenship — found overwhelming support for failed Democrat presidential candidate Kamala Harris over Trump.

A survey from the National Partnership for New Americans showed that 55 percent of naturalized citizen voters said they would “definitely” vote for Harris compared to just 41 percent who said the same about Trump.

By November 2023, the Biden administration had surged naturalizations so much that naturalized citizen voters would account for about 1-in-10 eligible voters in the 2024 election — giving enormous electoral power to Americans who were born outside the U.S.

In 2019, The Atlantic‘s Ronald Brownstein found that nearly 90 percent of House congressional districts with a foreign-born population above the national average are won by Democrats. This means every congressional district with a foreign-born population exceeding 15.5 percent has a 90 percent chance of electing Democrats and only a 10 percent chance of electing Republicans.

The Washington Post, the New York Times, the Atlantic, Axios, the Los Angeles Times, and the Wall Street Journal have all admitted that rapid demographic shifts spurred by mass immigration are tilting the nation toward a permanent Democrat political majority.

John Binder is a reporter for Breitbart News. Email him at jbinder@breitbart.com.
news.summary:
Socialist Melat Kiros (D), who unseated 15-term Rep. Diana DeGette (D) in Colorado’s first congressional district Democrat primary last month, says the United States must elect more immigrants specifically from the "Global South" to usher in a "reckoning" with the global world order.

news.title: Democrats are having internal problems play out: Ben Domenech
news.text:
Fox News contributor Ben Domenech analyzes the internal problems of the Democratic Party, focusing on the divide between socialist and establishment candidates.

Fox News contributor Ben Domenech joins Stuart Varney to discuss the Democratic Party's internal conflicts, particularly the choice between socialist and establishment candidates in races like Maine. Domenech also addresses New York Magazine's declaration of Kamala Harris as the front-runner for 2028, expressing skepticism given her past campaign's challenges and blaming consultants for her previous loss.
news.summary: Fox News contributor Ben Domenech analyzes the internal problems of the Democratic Party, focusing on the divide between socialist and establishment candidates.

news.title: AOC mocked by MAGA over her use of ‘southern accent’ in Tennessee rally speech
news.text:
New York Democratic Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez has been mocked by MAGA conservatives over her apparent adoption of a southern accent while delivering a speech in Tennessee.

The progressive addressed supporters in Memphis Friday but one particular moment sparked interest when it was shared on social media. 

“We cannot afford to leave Tennessee behind!” Ocasio-Cortez says from behind a podium backing Justin J Pearson for Congress. “We cannot afford to leave Kentucky behind, Alabama behind, Mississippi behind, Georgia behind, South Carolina behind!”

A slight drawl noticeable in her repeated use of the word “behind” led pundit Benny Johnson to ridicule her on Sean Hannity’s Fox News show Monday.

AOC had been stumping for State Rep.Pearson in the clip and went on to do the same for Michigan Senate candidate Abdul El-Sayed in Lansing and Grand Rapids over the weekend, prompting Johnson to tell Hannity: “You know, I’m surprised AOC is not trying to speak in an Arabic dialect when she is in Michigan.

“This is a theater kid-occupied government, Sean. We have been warning about this for a long time. It’s called live action role playing, LARPing. These people don’t know who they are or where they are, they will change their accents. 

“It’s this change of your accent I can’t get over. Barack Obama does this, Hillary Clinton famously does this.”

Pivoting to Hannity directly, Johnson asked: “Do you do this? You have millions of viewers across the south, you have millions of viewers in Texas, but I have never seen you do this show in a southern drawl, Sean. Why is that?”

The broadcaster said he had never adopted a southern accent, causing Johnson to speculate that Democrats “don’t know who they are”.

“The fundamental difference is these people don’t believe anything,” he said. “They don’t know who they are, they don’t know where they are from, and they don’t know what they stand for. 

“People that know who they are, they don’t have to pander. You don’t use a Chinese accent when you go to a Chinese restaurant because you know who you are and where you’re from.”

Growing into his theme, the influencer said: “Conservatives have a fundamental bedrock of who we are as people, an ego, an ethos. 

“We are Christian, we are American, we don’t have to pander, we are proud of where we are from, we are not ashamed of it. These people have to change their tune everywhere they go because they actually don’t believe in anything other than power, and power is the fundamental goal. 

“In order to get power you want to try to bring those audiences to your side, so you change your accent and you change your affect in order to do that. It is very cringe.”

Other right-wingers accusing Ocasio-Cortez of inauthenticity included Glenn Greenwald, who claimed she had used “a fake preacher accent” and Scott Jennings, who presented examples of Clinton and Kamala Harris behaving similarly and called the trio “performers”, “actors” and “fakers.”

AOC has yet to comment on the response to her speech.

EXECUTIVE NEWS SUMMARY

Anti-ICE protester injures immigrant
If leftists want to be seen by others the way they see themselves — i.e., as the benevolent nobility protecting the little man (in this case, illegal immigrants) — they should probably take care not to firebomb those immigrants. On Monday morning, Andrew Arrabaca, an “anti-ICE rioter,” allegedly ignited fireworks and set a fire outside 26 Federal Plaza in Lower Manhattan, a building that houses multiple federal agencies, including ICE’s New York City headquarters. After being detained, Arrabaca, a U.S. Army veteran, said he was targeting the building and that it was okay if both federal employees and civilians were harmed. As it turned out, only one person was injured: an immigrant who was appearing for an immigration status hearing. The cart Arrabaca used to transport his materials to the scene had a sign reading “ICE off our streets.”

Teen dead in Ayanna Pressley’s husband’s home
On Saturday, an 18-year-old woman was found shot to death inside a Boston house owned by Democrat Congresswoman Ayanna Pressley’s husband, Conan Harris. The young woman has been identified as Yoseliani Marte via a GoFundMe campaign launched by her mother. According to the campaign, Marte was a victim of “domestic violence.” Neither Pressley nor Harris live at the residence in question, which is reportedly a rental property valued at $1.15 million. Curiously, the mainstream media has been entirely uncurious about the incident, especially given the fact that Harris is a convicted felon who spent 10 years in prison for drug trafficking. If Pressley were a Republican, the media coverage of this incident would not be so indifferent and dismissive.

Canadian tariffs
President Donald Trump has invoked the Tariff Act of 1930 to impose a 50% tariff against a range of Canadian goods. According to Trump’s proclamation, “The United States, U.S. businesses and workers, and U.S. commerce suffer from Canada’s discriminatory, unequal, and unreasonable tariff scheme.” Trump’s gripe is over Canada’s current trade policy that unfairly targets three U.S. sectors — vehicles, dairy, and alcohol — negatively impacting American sales while advantaging other foreign competitors. This move, according to the Trump administration, is not intended to start a trade war with Canada; rather, the tariffs “are defensive measures by the United States taken to remedy discriminatory actions by Canada.” Canada’s discriminatory policies against American goods have resulted in declining sales. For example, the imposition of restrictions on U.S. alcohol exports has dropped sales of American alcohol in Canada by 81% since March 2025.

Nuns, New York, and hospice euthanasia
New York Democrat Governor Kathy Hochul sees the euthanasia MAID program in Canada as a roadmap and is trying to force Catholic nuns to participate. Thirteen plaintiffs, including several orders of Catholic nuns, have filed suit to stop New York’s Medical Aid in Dying program, which currently lacks a proper exception for religious belief. Hochul insists that the bill does protect religious liberty since it includes protections against coercion for patients and does not require a religious organization to “offer” euthanasia. The nuns say the religious protections are not enough since they only allow the nuns to refuse to prescribe or administer the lethal drugs. These dedicated Christian women would still be required to allow doctors and nurses to counsel patients on MAID and walk them through the entire approval process while the patients are under the sisters’ care.

Talarico clashes with black voters
James Talarico, Democrat candidate for Texas Senate, answered questions for 90 minutes at his Sunday town hall meeting. He said he needed the support of the black community to win the Senate race and pledged $25 million in voter outreach. But Kandice Webber, a Black Lives Matter activist and organizer, confronted him, claiming he had no real interest in improving their quality of life. “You’re not leaving here until you answer our questions,” Webber said. “Black people don’t trust you for this reason, and you cannot win without us, so you stay here, you answer our questions, or you suffer the consequences.” Recent polling suggests over 60% of black voters support Talarico — an unusually low number for a Democrat.

Dept. of Education has a new college standard
With student debt ballooning into the trillions, President Trump’s Department of Education is establishing an accountability test on colleges. Institutions that fail to prove their graduates earn more than comparable workers will lose access to federal student loans. The Department of Education will evaluate colleges over three years, and programs must meet earning benchmarks in two of those years. This accountability test should better protect both students and taxpayers. “For too long, badly calibrated financial incentives have driven universities to squeeze tuition revenue from taxpayers and young, impressionable individuals instead of preparing them for rewarding and remunerative careers,” said Education Secretary Linda McMahon. It’s no wonder college enrollment is declining. The average cost of college for a four-year degree in the United States is over $150,000.

U.S. comes to Venezuela’s rescue, expands food security efforts
The U.S. State Department has worked with the U.S. Department of Agriculture and the U.S. military to help stabilize Venezuela, which was rocked by two massive earthquakes in June, leaving almost 4,000 people killed and more than 16,000 injured. In the initial aftermath, the U.S. pledged $150 million in humanitarian aid to Venezuela, which has already grown to over $180 million. The State Department has provided food, while the USDA has coordinated food security efforts, and the military has handled distribution. Venezuela’s acting president, Delcy Rodriguez, has worked closely with the U.S. State Department since her predecessor was captured by the U.S. in January.

Where is Mojtaba Khamenei?
Iran’s new supreme leader, who replaced his father Ayatollah Ali Khamenei after he was killed in a joint U.S.-Israeli airstrike, has not been seen in public since he was named the new leader. There are now rumors swirling that Khamenei is not even in Iran, but fled the country and may be currently hiding out in Russia, a longtime ally of the Iranian regime. The excuse for Khamenei not to show his face in public is the legitimate concern that doing so would compromise his security and hidden location, inviting a strike from the Israelis. Furthermore, Khamenei is believed to have suffered significant injuries from U.S.-Israeli strikes, and may be severely disfigured. However, others question the claim that Khamenei left Iran, noting that the country still has medical facilities that could meet Khamenei’s needs, and leaving could undermine his tenuous hold on power.

Treasury stops $100M payment to the dead
Secretary Scott Bessent’s Department of the Treasury has initially reviewed 885 million benefit payments totaling over $2.7 trillion and already found and stopped almost $100 million in payments going to dead people. As the review continues, Bessent estimates up to $350 million can be saved by the end of the year. The review is being conducted as part of a new program that gives the Treasury access to Social Security’s “Full Death Master File.” Access was initially granted on a three-year trial basis before being made permanent by the Ending Improper Payments to Deceased People Act, which President Trump signed into law in February. These numbers are still a drop in the bucket compared to the U.S. annual budget, but the savings already triple those from the pilot program, which totaled only $31 million.
