news.title: WNBA All-Star charity initiative supports sports group that says it is ‘moving beyond the binary of girl’
news.text: The WNBA is celebrating its milestone 30th season with a new All-Star initiative that the league says is designed to help keep girls involved in sports. But one of the Chicago-area organizations selected to benefit from the league’s $100,000 All-Star prize pool says on its own website that it is "moving beyond the binary of ‘girl’" and welcomes "gender-expansive participants regardless of gender identity or expression." The organization is Girls in the Game, the charity affiliated with "Team Spoon" in the 2026 WNBA All-Star Game. According to the league’s July 15 release, the WNBA All-Star teams will compete for a $100,000 prize pool benefiting two Chicago-area organizations "dedicated to keeping girls involved in sports." The winning team’s organization receives $70,000, while the runner-up’s organization gets $30,000. Team Coop is playing for Angels Athletics, while Team Spoon is supporting Girls in the Game. MEGAN RAPINOE'S LATEST DEFENSE OF MALES IN WOMEN'S SPORTS COLLAPSES UNDER ITS OWN HYPOCRISY The WNBA described Girls in the Game as an organization that "partners with schools, parks, and community centers in Chicago to offer sports, health, and leadership programs." That sounds straightforward enough until you dig deeper into the Girls in the Game website. OutKick/Fox News Digital reviewed the organization’s website and found language that raises obvious questions about how Girls in the Game defines "girl." Under the heading "We stand for inclusivity," Girls in the Game says: "Moving beyond the binary of ‘girl’, we welcome LGBTQ+ youth and gender-expansive participants regardless of gender identity or expression." Wait, what? Girls in the Game is not a generic youth charity that happens to work with girls. The organization repeatedly frames its mission around girls, girls’ sports and girls-only spaces. So when it says it is "moving beyond the binary of ‘girl,’" people deserve to know what that actually means. OutKick/Fox News Digital has not been able to confirm whether any biological males have participated in Girls in the Game’s girl-centered sports programming. According to its 2025 annual report, Girls in the Game served 3,059 participants in FY25. The report says its Chicago After School program alone served 921 participants and girls "play sports, learn about healthy lifestyle choices, and improve their leadership skills in an all-girl setting." The report also says the program provides a "safe, non-competitive environment for girls to learn and be physically active." The organization’s public-facing program materials do not clearly state whether participation is based on biological sex, gender identity or another eligibility standard. But Girls in the Game’s own LGBTQ resource pages also point users toward gender-identity-related services. One page lists Howard Brown as a resource that includes "gender-affirming health care" and "trans and non-binary care," along with a support group intended for "BIPOC, trans, non-conforming, questioning individuals" and the Trans Lifeline hotline. Another Girls in the Game LGBTQ resource page lists "Gender Affirming clothing, makeup, community resources," along with God’s Closet, which it describes as offering "clothes and tailoring for gender-nonconforming/trans people." That page also lists a support group for "BIPOC trans, non-conforming, questioning individuals" and Trans Lifeline. MARTINA NAVRATILOVA SAYS SHE TRIED TO WARN KAMALA HARRIS' CAMPAIGN ABOUT TRANS ATHLETES: 'NOBODY WOULD LISTEN' If the WNBA is promoting this as an initiative to keep girls involved in sports, and if one of the beneficiaries says it is moving beyond the binary definition of "girl," then the league and the organization need to explain who is eligible for those programs. OutKick/Fox News Digital reached out to Girls in the Game and asked whether biological males who "identify" as girls, transgender girls, "nonbinary" or "gender-expansive" are eligible to participate in its girl-centered sports programming. OutKick/Fox News Digital also asked whether any Girls in the Game programs operate as girls-only spaces, whether those programs are limited based on biological sex or gender identity, whether parents are told before enrollment that the organization is "moving beyond the binary of ‘girl,’" and whether the WNBA All-Star money would be unrestricted or directed toward a specific program. Girls in the Game did not respond to multiple requests for comment. OutKick/Fox News Digital also contacted Angels Athletics, the organization affiliated with Team Coop, to ask whether its girls basketball and girls sports programs are limited to biological girls or open based on gender identity. Angels Athletics also did not respond to multiple requests for comment. Then there’s the WNBA. OutKick/Fox News Digital asked the league whether it was aware of Girls in the Game’s "moving beyond the binary of ‘girl’" language before selecting the organization for the All-Star initiative. OutKick/Fox News Digital also asked whether the league believes an organization that says it is moving beyond the binary definition of "girl" fits an initiative described as keeping girls involved in sports, whether the WNBA asked Girls in the Game about eligibility for biological males who identify as girls or "gender-expansive" participants, and whether the WNBA supports biological males participating in girls’ youth sports programming if they "identify" as girls. The WNBA did not respond to OutKick/Fox News Digital’s request for comment. Maybe there’s a simple explanation. Girls in the Game might argue that its programming is non-competitive or centered on youth development rather than formal leagues. But that wouldn’t answer the important question. The WNBA did not announce a prize-pool initiative to help keep "gender-expansive" youth involved in sports. It said the money would benefit organizations dedicated to keeping girls involved in sports. Girls in the Game, meanwhile, describes some of its own programming as taking place in an "all-girl setting," while also saying it is "moving beyond the binary of ‘girl’" and welcomes participants regardless of gender identity or expression. EDUCATION SECRETARY LINDA MCMAHON RIPS CALIFORNIA TRANS ATHLETE ‘COMPROMISE,’ TELLS NEWSOM TO ‘PICK A SIDE’ Whether the sports are competitive or non-competitive is not the only issue. The issue is that an organization built around girls’ sports spaces is openly advertising that those spaces are not necessarily just for girls. As for the WNBA, maybe the league will say it selected the organization for its broader youth-development work and never asked about eligibility because the money is not tied to competitive girls’ sports. These are not difficult questions. This whole mess could be cleaned up with a simple email response. Instead, Girls in the Game, Angels Athletics and the WNBA have not provided one. CLICK HERE FOR MORE SPORTS COVERAGE ON FOXNEWS.COM SIGN UP TO GET THE LATEST SPORTS NEWS The WNBA is using its All-Star Game, one of its biggest events of the season, to promote an initiative framed around "keeping girls involved in sports." The league wants credit for helping keep girls in sports, a noble endeavor to be sure. But before the commissioner’s office pats itself on the back too hard, it should probably explain what it means by "girls."
news.summary: A WNBA All-Star beneficiary says it is "moving beyond the binary of 'girl'" while the league frames the $100,000 prize pool as keeping girls in sports.
news.author: Dan Zaksheske

news.title: Ted Cruz Agrees with Kamala Harris’ Comment About Election Security
news.text:
Republican Senator Ted Cruz (R-Texas) announced Monday that he agrees with a plan to enhance election integrity promoted by former Democrat vice president and senator Kamala Harris.

“Agreed,” Sen. Cruz wrote on X.com, replying to the following March 31, 2019 post by then-Senator Harris calling for the passage of a bill requiring votes be cast through paper ballots:

“Paper ballots are the smartest, safest way to ensure your vote is secure against attacks by foreign actors. Russia can’t hack a piece of paper like they can a computer. We introduced the Secure Elections Act to ensure our elections are safeguarded. We must be ready.”

As a California senator, Harris co-sponsored Democrats’ “Election Security Act of 2019” to require paper ballots. At the time, she publicly stressed the need for the paper ballot requirement, both on television programs such as ABC’s “The View” and during press events.

Today, however, Harris is aggressively opposing Republicans’ SAVE America Act, which would require voters to provide proof of citizenship and photo identification in order to validate their eligibility to cast ballots.

Such safeguards against voter fraud are tantamount to voter suppression, Harris claimed in a July 16, 2026 X.com post:

“The SAVE Act (sic)is voter suppression. It is part of a larger agenda of conservatives trying to steal power from the people.”

The SAVE America Act passed the Republican-controlled House in February, but has been held up by Senate Democrats’ filibuster ever since. As a result, Republicans have resorted to folding key elements of their effort to ensure that only legal voters vote in U.S. elections into a funding bill, through a process known as reconciliation. Reconciliation requires approval by only a simple majority of the Senate, instead of the two-thirds vote needed to overcome a filibuster.

news.title: Football, golf … behold the future of world sport: Donald Trump as referee-in-chief | Marina Hyde
news.text:
For so many of us around the world, the final scenes of the World Cup were a chance to switch off the TV with an eye-roll and observe mildly: “Well, I think that’s quite enough America for us all for a while now.” And yet: there can and must always be more. It is simply the way of things. If you thought the World Cup being carried on to the pitch ahead of Sunday’s final in some kind of Louis Vuitton sex trunk was the nadir of hysterical trashiness in sport, then simply recall the lesson of the past few years and expand your mind. It can always get trashier.

To the parched fairways of Royal Birkdale, then, the host of this year’s Open Championship, which – yesssssss! – was won by someone other than Bryson DeChambeau or Scottie Scheffler or any of the other radioactively ghastly US golfers you’d cheerfully head-flush in a Mar-a-Lago loo just as long as you could clear the classified documents out of it first. It has now been revealed that, during his heated row over a two-shot penalty he was handed for improving the area for his backswing, DeChambeau has been accused of threatening to call the US president unless it was all resolved in his favour. To repeat: threatening to call Donald Trump.

If you missed this, DeChambeau picked up the penalty for improving the line of his swing, apparently having trodden down some long grass close to his ball on the fifth hole during the second round last Friday. He publicly lost his mind about it, demanded to be driven back to the scene with officials, then returned to the tournament scoring area and reportedly threatened to call Trump. Has a new portal to the dark side opened in sport? Given Trump’s known intervention in the World Cup (of which more shortly), who among us would bet against it?

At time of writing, I had read everything about this sensational escalation, but I still don’t know nearly enough about it. I need every detail. I need everyone involved to reconstruct the entire conversation. I want to know exactly how quickly you might go from “no way did I do any gardening” to “fuck you – patch in the president”. This is a new era of American pant-wetting and I need to understand absolutely everything about its conventions.

Certainly, all I already thought about Bryson DeChambeau has been magnified. If you’re unfamiliar with his work, as well as being a two-time US Open champion, Bryson is a hardcore Mar-a-Lago steak-gobbler who got a shout-out in Trump’s 2024 election victory address, though that was delivered at the Palm Beach Convention Center and not at the latter’s Floridian golf-club residence. (Even so, every time we see those words, the hilarity should strike us anew. The president of the United States of America lives in a golf club. Honestly: I just CAN’T.) Anyway, in the small hours after his victory over Kamala Harris, Trump was very keen to pluck DeChambeau from the sliders queue or the ormolu urinals or wherever and get him up on stage. “Bryson DeChambeau is up here somewhere,” Trump told the cameras. “What happened to Bryson? Where is he? Bryson! He’s hitting balls. He’s on the way?” Eventually, DeChambeau realised there was nothing for it but to climb awkwardly on to the stage.

And yet, where is your orange god now, Bryson? Where was he on Friday, as you seemingly failed to summon him to a faraway British links course having been observed doing a bit of landscaping?

There seems, at present, to be no evidence that Bryson put the call in, and since he flaked out of all media appearances for the tournament, no one has been able to ask if he contacted the president in any other way. We understand, however, that he asked for Trump to be called. Sad. Perhaps the only thing worse than demanding that the president be called over you dicking around with the grass is not having the president called over you dicking around with the grass. And then realising that, in many ways, you got less off Trump than some guy called Folarin Balogun who plays a sport called … hang on, let me get my lorgnette … “soccer”. That would be a lot of perspective for Bryson to absorb on a Friday lunchtime.

The maddest thing about the entire revelation is what it says about how comically debased and politicised sport has been in such short order. Because the thing is, you can totally see it. You can genuinely imagine that the only situation room Trump wouldn’t lose focus in after about 10 minutes is one where he was required to cheat remotely at golf. You can just picture him assembling Pete Hegseth and Marco Rubio and all the other guys with the too-big shoes in the White House situation room, and them huddling round a feed like the photo of the Obama administration watching the Seal team raid that got Osama bin Laden. Only this one is coming from a grass-level camera at Royal Birkdale.

As indicated, we already know the US president cheats at sport for people. Precisely two weeks ago, this column covered the business of him making three phone calls to the Fifa president, Gianni Infantino, about the red card and match suspension handed to US striker Balogun. On Sunday, the Uefa president, Aleksander Ceferin, went so far as to boycott the World Cup final over, among other things, Infantino allowing this. The only question, surely, is when the next incident or threat will occur. The floodgates of Whitehousery have been opened, and only the US has the power to shut them.

 Marina Hyde is a Guardian columnist

news.title: Bor-ing! CNN Dumps on Sen. Mark Kelly's Presidential Prospects in 2028
news.text: CNN This Morning7/21/266:36 am EDTAUDIE CORNISH: So next, I want to turn to 2028. Arizona Senator Mark Kelly is being talked about, at least in a new article from The Atlantic. It talks about him as, quote, a wingman. The article points to his resume and the goodwill he has accrued as the spouse of Gabby Giffords. Kelly is a retired Navy pilot, as well as a former astronaut. He's an outspoken critic of President Trump and the administration. The Group Chat is back. He's got a solid resume. Right? Can you talk about-- but he was vetted at one point for vice president, what happened there? 'Cause he didn't get that one.EDWARD ISAAC DOVERE: Well, that was 2024, that very rushed process, and he was one of the three finalists. He had an interview with Kamala Harris, but obviously he wasn't the one picked. . . . CHUCK ROCHA: Most folks who run for president, I don't remember anybody ever getting elected president because of their policy portfolio, or we would have a President Elizabeth Warren. Remember, I have a policy for that? Presidents are elected on different thing. This about being a fighter? Think about a Barack Obama, inspirational. Think about Donald Trump, listen to the Democrat here, 'cause he would inspire new people to show up. That's the part in the political operative mind that Kelly's missing. He has maybe the most perfect resume ever, but is he inspirational on the stump? CORNISH: Yeah, so a perfect resume plus 25 million dollars.ASHLEY DAVIS: Yeah, and 25 million dollars these days for a political campaign is nothing for president.But I, I also think that you're exactly right. I don't know if he has that oomph. I mean, like, obviously people like AOC brings people out to the stump and listen to them. Here's my thing. I'm not convinced, Isaac, that people running in 2028 run against Trump, and that's gonna be enough. Like, Trump's gone. DOVERE: No, I don't think it will be enough. I think, but it will be part of the calculation for Democrats, and but part of it is also gonna be who can inspire them, who can make them feel like there is a page to turn. And look, Mark Kelly is not the most dynamic speaker --DAVID: Right, that's --DOVERE: -- to Chuck's point. And, and can he become that person? Part of it is that he's a Navy guy, an astronaut. That's what he did. He was very careful about everything.CORNISH: Out of the 45 men who have been US presidents, 31 have had a military record or have served. He is a decorated former veteran. I think it's interesting that right now, Democrats are looking to all these dudes who have jobs that you say you wanna have when you grow up, when you're a six year old boy. It's like, I'm gonna be an astronaut, I'm gonna cut down trees, you know what I mean? Yeah, I'm gonna be a fisherman. Like, it feels as though in the hunt for manly men, like, if there's anybody who, you know, has that energy is kind of an option.. . . DAVIS: If the Democratic establishment sees AOC or someone like that taking off, and they want someone boring [Cornish laughs] 'cause the country is tired of the, you know, Trump days, Kelly is that stable --CORNISH: The standard for boring has changed so much. DOVERE: Boring -- he went to space, his wife was almost assassinated --DAVIS: Yeah, I don't mean, but you know what, he's not like --CORNISH: He's not that boring actually. DAVIS: You know my point.CORNISH: But yeah, yeah, less chaos, less chaos for sure.ROCHA: If there's just two people running, that's the point. But if there are six, you can win this thing with 30% in a primary.DAVIS: But does the establishment get behind you -- or Bernie?
news.summary:
CNN This Morning 7-21-26
      
  
Mark Kelly is a uniter! — just not in the way a prospective presidential candidate would want to be.Commenting on news that the Democratic Senator from Arizona is considering a 2028 White House bid, today's CNN This Morning Group Chat was "united" in a blunt consensus: Kelly boasts a sterling resume, but lacks the spark to inspire voters. Bald, straight, white male. The concurrence was brutal. Panelists described the retired Navy pilot and former astronaut as "not the most dynamic speaker," missing that elusive "oomph," lacking the "inspirational" element past winners had, and even being "boring." Ouch!At the end of the segment, CNN reporter Edward-Isaac Dovere, perhaps atoning for having branded Kelly "not the most dynamic speaker,” tried to make the case that he is not that boring after all, pointing to his past as an astronaut, and being the husband of the nearly-assassinated Gabby Giffords. But those are biographical points—they don't speak to Kelly's bland personality.H

news.title: Kamala Harris is the 'fairly obvious' 2028 presidential frontrunner, Democratic insiders say
news.text: Multiple Democratic aides and strategists suggested Monday that former Vice President Kamala Harris is the "fairly obvious" frontrunner for the 2028 Democratic presidential nomination. New York Magazine's Intelligencer spoke to several Harris aides regarding her potential chances if she chooses to enter the race after her defeat in the 2024 presidential election. Despite her loss, some anonymous Democratic supporters believe, according to the report, that Harris has the upper hand over other party candidates based on her name recognition and recent public appearances. DEMOCRATS WISH KAMALA HARRIS RAN FOR CALIFORNIA GOVERNOR, NOT LOOK TO 2028: REPORT "She walks in as the front-runner. It is fairly obvious she is followed wherever she goes," one former aide said. "And I don’t want to upset any narratives that any of the other candidates are pushing, but she comes in with 99% name ID and everyone else is going to have to raise money and buy it." "She is looking for where the wave is going and is determined to ride it," another supporter said. "Her mindset is that I should have won, and I am going to do it my way because my way would have won, and it was all these consultants that were telling me to take this position or that position, and it was their fault." Though some were concerned that Harris could be bogged down by her past support for the Biden administration and controversial viewpoints, others suggested that voters will be more forgiving of the former vice president after the second Trump administration. CARVILLE SHREDS DEMS' 2024 AUTOPSY, SAYS HARRIS CAMPAIGN WAS 'MOST INEFFECTIVE $2 BILLION EVER SPENT' "Look how quickly voters forgot about Trump’s first term," an anonymous Democratic official said. "People have short attention spans and short memories, and for all the criticisms that could be directed at Biden today, who knows what they think 12 or 16 months from now." However, not every person who spoke with the Intelligencer was optimistic about Harris' chances or whether she should even run again in 2028. One New York donor expressed doubts that she could convince major donors that she could win the White House. "I hope she runs," Democratic strategist Alex Hoffman said. "Because then she will lose, and we will never have to hear from her again. She will never win an election at a national scale. Never." JOY BEHAR REJECTS KAMALA HARRIS FOR 2028, TELLS 'THE VIEW' CO-HOSTS THEY'RE NOT LIVING IN 'REALITY' Fox News Digital reached out to Harris' office for comment. Harris has repeatedly suggested that she is open to the idea of running for president again and has made appearances in several key swing states over the past year. "I might. I might. I’m thinking about it… I’ll keep you posted," Harris said at the National Action Network’s 35th Anniversary Convention in April. CLICK HERE TO DOWNLOAD THE FOX NEWS APP The Intelligencer report came amid speculation that Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, D-N.Y., could launch a 2028 presidential campaign, an idea the New York representative teased last month.
news.summary: Democratic aides called former Vice President Kamala Harris the "fairly obvious" 2028 presidential race frontrunner choice in an Intelligencer report.

news.title: Daniel McCarthy: Why Democrats Can't Do Better Than Kamala Harris in 2028
news.text:
There are only two lanes to the Democratic presidential nomination two years from now, and one of them is already blocked — by Kamala Harris.

Get ready for the Kamala comeback, followed, in all likelihood, by a replay of her crushing defeat by the Republican nominee.

Believe it or not, the ex-VP occupies what counts as the “center lane” within the party, and for most of the last three decades that’s the one that’s led to the nomination.

Republicans used to be the party of orderly succession, even if the candidate whose turn had come looked like a certain loser: think Bob Dole in ‘96.

But recently, it’s been Democrats who’ve tended to pick a party elder as their nominee:

Al Gore, as a sitting VP, might not have been remarkable in 2000, but in '04 it was John Kerry, in '16 Hillary Clinton and in 2020 Joe Biden — with only Barack Obama butting ahead of the line in 2008.

Democrats like a known quantity, a reliable liberal who isn’t too overtly radical; the radicalism can wait until after Election Day.

So they’re leaning to running Harris again, even if donors aren’t happy about it: They blew nearly $2 billion on her campaign and aligned PAC’s in 2024, only for Harris to lose every battleground state to Trump.

It won’t be donors who decide the party’s 2028 nominee, though, and ordinary Democrats actually find Harris reassuring.

She’s a familiar face, after all, commanding practically universal name recognition.

Harris checks the identity-politics boxes that may be as important as winning to Democratic primary voters: Their ticket must have a woman, and it can’t be all white.

And after being vetted as a VP and undergoing the trial-by-fire of the '24 campaign, Harris isn’t likely to surprise voters with any extremist tattoos or sexual-assault allegations — things that can’t be taken for granted among Democrats nowadays.

Then there’s the revenge motive: What better way to repudiate the Trump era and reverse the '24 humiliation than by making the woman he defeated president?

Democratic primary voters are still smarting from Hillary Clinton’s drubbing in 2016: by 2028, the election of America’s first female president will have been postponed 12 long years, and feminists in the party aren’t willing to wait any longer.

These are the reasons Harris tops almost all polls of 2028 Democratic hopefuls, with an 11.6-point lead in the RealClearPolitics polling aggregate as of mid-July.

“She comes in with 99 percent name ID and everyone else is going to have to raise money and buy it,” a former Harris aide tells New York’s David Freedlander.

The donors might prefer just about anyone else, yet they’d have to pour an enormous amount of cash into another candidate just to make him or her recognizable.

This has led one longtime Democratic strategist with donors’ ears to welcome the inevitable: “I hope she runs,” Alex Hoffman told New York, “Because then she will lose and we will never have to hear from her again. She will never win an election at a national scale. Never.”

But if Harris is hopeless, why does she seem inevitable?

She has seniority no one else can match, but what about the other lane that leads to the nomination?

That’s the left lane — the far left lane.

The one Bernie Sanders occupied in 2020 and 2016, and that a candidate like Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez might fill in 2028.

Young Dems keep moving leftward, with the Democratic Socialists of America making deeper inroads into the party.

Harris is not nearly left-wing enough for them — but then, anyone who is would be upsetting to the old guard.

It’s not that the old-timers aren’t also well to the left on economics, but they don’t want to be labeled “socialists” — while the insurgent left is proud of the brand.

The party’s mainstream wants to have it both ways on the Israel-Palestine conflict — while the young left, increasingly, is explicit about its anti-Zionism.

There are still enough baby boomers and suburbanites in the Democratic Party to make the left lane an unlikely road to the nomination. For now.

Harris can expect a challenge from the left, but the party is still probably one more presidential cycle away from nominating someone who openly represents where Democrats are headed.

In fact, a Harris defeat in 2028 would be the perfect prelude to the socialist and anti-Zionist left’s takeover, discrediting the old guard for forfeiting the election to Donald Trump’s successor.

Insider Democrats know Kamala Harris is a terrible candidate, but the party they built can’t tell her no — even if this is the last nomination they get to make before DSA takes over.

news.title: NYT piece indicts Trump's expanding wave of policy failures
news.text:
In 2024, one of the Trump campaign's most effective lines of attack against Democratic nominee Kamala Harris was, "Kamala is for they/them. President Trump is for you." The argument was that while Democrats were obsessed with language policing and pronouns, Donald Trump was focused on real issues like inflation, jobs and the high cost of living. And that messaging worked, enabling Trump to pull off a narrow victory.

But 18 months into his second presidency, Trump is suffering from weak approval ratings in poll after poll. Political scientists, interviewed by The New York Times' Thomas Edsall, laid out some of the many reasons for Trump's unpopularity.

Trump's "kiss of death," Edsall argues in a Tuesday column for the Times, ranges from "job losses" and his "imposition of tariffs" to a "record surge in the national debt" and "the economic havoc resulting from his war against Iran."

Matthew Dallek, who teaches political history at George Washington University in Washington, DC, told Edsall, "Governing via conspiracy theories, personal whim and wish-casting, Trump has taken his place as the most consistently inept president of this century. At home and abroad, in many Cabinet and sub-Cabinet agencies, the administration's culture is dominated by incompetence."

Michael Bailey, a political science professor at Georgetown University in Washington, DC, cites Trump's refusal to listen to expert advice as a major flaw of his second presidency.

Bailey told Edsall, "Many presidents have made mistakes, but none have made mistakes so cavalierly as President Trump. He makes rash, reckless decisions; at best, they are based on barstool theories of how the world works — and at worst, they are driven by corrupt motives. Since he 'just knows' he is right about how tariffs work, no evidence will sway him. In the short term, the country has been, in many ways, resilient. But he is corroding state capacity, democratic norms and the very sense of a unified nation."

But while Bailey is highly critical of Trump from a policy standpoint, believes that the president is "quite skilled as a political leader" and notes his "hold on the Republican primary electorate."

Donald Moynihan, who teaches public policy at the University of Michigan, told Edsall, "There are three categories of Trump failures: disasters of mismanagement, disasters by design and disasters of greed. What are the achievements of the second Trump presidency thus far? Persistent inflation, sluggish economic growth, an unpopular war, aggressive immigration enforcement that has outraged many and the shredding of the safety net to fund a large tax cut…. Trump no longer feels the need to be bound by precedent, norms or good advice."

Rottinghaus told Edsall, "What's different about Trump is that many of his supporters evaluate him less by traditional policy outcomes and more by whether he's fighting the political and cultural battles they care about. In today's polarized environment, political identity often outweighs measurable policy success or failure…. What distinguishes Trump is his willingness to pursue high-risk, high-visibility policies with relatively little concern for expert consensus or institutional caution. That increases both the potential payoff and the potential for costly failures."

news.title: How the Michigan Senate primary activated every bitter Democratic grudge
news.text:
A decade’s worth of grudges and feuds between the Democratic establishment and the insurgent left is coming to a head in one election this year: Michigan’s Senate primary.

Key takeaways
* Many long-simmering grudges between the establishment Democratic Party faction and a faction supporting Sen. Bernie Sanders have come to the fore in Michigan’s Senate primary.
* Dynamics involving gender and race, concerns over electability, and claims of establishment malfeasance closely mirror dynamics in Sanders’s two presidential runs.
* Every intra-Democratic controversy over Israel — from the influence of AIPAC to claims of antisemitism — has come up too, showcasing a party with some bitter divides.

The contest between Rep. Haley Stevens and Abdul El-Sayed has somehow unearthed every fault line that’s divided the two factions over the years — from gender and racial politics, to Israel and Gaza, to questions of electability and fair play, to money in politics, and much more.
On one side there’s Stevens, a four-term House member who is the preferred candidate of the Democratic establishment — and of AIPAC, which has spent nearly $30 million to support her campaign. 
On the other is El-Sayed, a former public health official who’s been backed by Sen. Bernie Sanders, Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, and their network of supporters and fans. 
But the race has quickly gone beyond just them and become about something bigger — the national struggle for influence in the Democratic Party. 
“This is the quintessential contest between the establishment wing of the Democratic Party and the economically populist wing,” said Joseph Geevarghese, executive director of the left group Our Revolution.
In contrast, Jonathan Cowan, president of the center-left group Third Way, argued that nominating El-Sayed — a “super extreme candidate who will alienate lots of voters in the middle” — is a dangerous risk.
In this one race, the Democratic factions are relitigating the 2016 presidential primary, the 2020 presidential primary, and several major controversies of the past few years, all at once. And whoever wins will have to find a way to somehow unite the party and win a competitive general election against presumptive Republican nominee Mike Rogers. 
On gender and racial politics, we’re doing 2016 again
[Image: Rep. Haley Stevens campaigns for the US Senate at the Michigan Democratic Nominating Convention in April 2026. https://platform.vox.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/2/2026/07/GettyImages-2272276506.jpg?quality=90&strip=all]
When Sanders challenged Hillary Clinton for the 2016 presidential nomination, her supporters frequently implied he had blind spots related to gender and race. They argued, for instance, that certain criticisms of Clinton (and remarks by Sanders) were sexist, and that Sanders’s poor performance with Black voters demonstrated the limits of his appeal. 
The discourse also sometimes focused on so-called “Bernie Bros” — online Sanders supporters who, critics argued, directed toxic abuse and vitriol toward people not in his camp. Sanders’s backers argued that the accusations of sexism were themselves a dishonest and inaccurate smear to distract from Clinton’s own shortcomings. 
All of this is happening again now in Michigan.
On gender, a comment from El-Sayed a few weeks back — he said Stevens was unable to “string together two coherent sentences” — was criticized by the Stevens camp as sexist. 
Then, last week, a video of an enthusiastic speech by Stevens on the campaign trail went viral as online El-Sayed supporters mocked her diction and delivery, viewing her way of speaking as self-evidently ridiculous. 
Seeking to tamp down this behavior, El-Sayed posted Friday that the way “people online” were making fun of Stevens “for things that have nothing to do with her policies” was “unkind and unhelpful.” He also told Vox’s America, Actually podcast that the accusations of him being disrespectful to women have gotten back to his daughter, and been hurtful.
[Media: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=WE9quwCWKuY]
Meanwhile, on race, a poll last week shows Black Democrats splitting 67 percent for Stevens, and 21 percent for El-Sayed. Days later, the Congressional Black Caucus PAC endorsed Stevens. Just like Clinton in 2016, Stevens is hoping support among Black voters who are longtime stalwarts of the Democratic base will be enough to carry her to victory. And just like Sanders in 2016 and 2020, El-Sayed is facing questions about whether the party’s left vanguard can expand its support beyond white, college-educated voters. 
“The progressive brand really, really underperforms with Black voters all around the country,” said Adrian Hemond of the Democratic advocacy firm Grassroots Midwest. “I think it’s worth Democrats, media, and people in the nonprofit blob asking themselves why that is.”
The establishment vs. insurgent tension, and the Obama of it all
[Image: Former President Barack Obama on June 18, 2026, in Chicago. https://platform.vox.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/2/2026/07/GettyImages-2282271139.jpg?quality=90&strip=all]
In both of Sanders’s presidential runs, his team griped about the behavior of the Democratic establishment, arguing that they weren’t truly playing fair. For instance, they pointed to how the DNC initially scheduled very few debates for the 2016 presidential contenders, arguing the supposedly neutral committee was in fact trying to help the frontrunner, Clinton, by limiting attention on her opponents.
To Sanders’s supporters, the bitterest pill was the perceived late intervention of former President Barack Obama into the 2020 campaign just as Sanders appeared well-positioned to win the nomination. (Days before the crucial Super Tuesday primaries, two other candidates, Pete Buttigieg and Amy Klobuchar, dropped out and endorsed Joe Biden. Obama was publicly neutral, but privately called Biden and Buttigieg during this key period, raising speculation about his role.)
In Michigan, again, the Democratic Senatorial Campaign Committee is officially neutral, but Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer has long been understood to favor Stevens, and reportedly has privately asked donors to back her.
Obama, too, has become a flashpoint. An AIPAC-linked group has spent millions on an ad showing Obama praising Stevens — even though he has not endorsed her in this race. The praise is from comments Obama made during Stevens’s first House campaign, in 2018. (Stevens had worked on his presidential task force to rescue the auto industry during the Great Recession.)
El-Sayed’s supporters have argued that this is an attempt to mislead Michigan voters into thinking Stevens has Obama’s backing. “It is disgusting that AIPAC — which spent tens of millions attacking Obama — is now spending like crazy to lie and suggest he endorsed Haley Stevens,” Ben Rhodes, a former Obama White House official who supports El-Sayed, posted on X.
Others suspect that Obama does in fact tacitly support Stevens, and that’s why he’s letting the ad stand without clarifying things. (El-Sayed thought Obama was too “centrist” and “middle of the road,” he told Vox in 2017.) And yet again, people are fighting over 2016: Some Stevens supporters point out that Sanders used footage of Obama praising him in a 2020 primary ad despite the senator’s own criticism of the former president. 
Israel/Gaza, AIPAC, antisemitism, money in politics, Hasan Piker, etc…
[Image: A pro-Palestine protest in Detroit in October 2024. https://platform.vox.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/2/2026/07/GettyImages-2176134164.jpg?quality=90&strip=all]
Of course, the issue that has come up repeatedly in tense Democratic primaries this cycle — Israel — is present in this race in a major way. 
Many on the left argue that the war in Gaza contributed to former Vice President Kamala Harris’s defeat by disillusioning some of her potential supporters and dissuading them from turning out. The evidence that this phenomenon occurred in significant numbers due to Gaza specifically is thin. But it arguably may have been a factor in Michigan, where 2 percent of the population is Muslim and Trump worked hard to court disaffected voters with vague promises to end the war.
Here, Stevens is an unapologetically pro-Israel candidate who wants to continue US military aid to the country and does not believe Israel committed genocide in Gaza. (She said during a Hanukkah event once that “Israel comes to me in my dreams.”) El-Sayed, in contrast, has been sharply critical of Israel throughout this race.
The center-left group Third Way has spent months attacking El-Sayed for campaigning with left-wing streamer Hasan Piker, arguing that comments of Piker’s — that “America deserved 9/11,” and that a certain sect of Israeli Orthodox Jews was “inbred” — are un-American and antisemitic. (Piker has said he calls lots of different groups he dislikes inbred.)
Meanwhile, Stevens has been the beneficiary of that nearly $30 million in AIPAC-linked spending — a topic she generally tries to avoid discussing. The ads in question don’t talk about Israel at all and instead praise Stevens on other issues. “It is just a clear indication of where the base of the party is right now that AIPAC has to continually do this spending through their allied outside groups,” Dani Negrete of Indivisible, a progressive group backing El-Sayed, told me.
Electability and the question of: who can’t win?
After a decade of factional squabbling, the left and the mainstream factions have each honed their talking points for why, when a Democrat loses the general election, it’s the other faction’s fault.
To the establishment, left candidates are risky bets: They’ve taken positions that may alienate swing voters and are less dependable than candidates with longer electoral pedigrees. 
In endorsing Stevens this month, Sen. Gary Peters (D-MI) warned they needed someone who was “fully vetted.” El-Sayed’s supporters viewed these and other remarks from Stevens supporters as baselessly linking their candidate to Graham Platner, who dropped out of his race in Maine over sexual assault allegations that he denies. However, unlike Platner, who was plucked from obscurity, El-Sayed has been a political figure for nearly a decade and faced significant media scrutiny already. 
Another version of the critique is more about whether El-Sayed’s policy positions — for instance, his past support for defunding the police, and his backing of Medicare-for-all — are too far left for this swing state in the general election.
Meanwhile, the left’s argument for why establishment candidates flop is that they fail to excite the base and stimulate turnout. Therefore, they claim, El-Sayed is the safer choice.
Lately, this argument has focused on Israel and Gaza. Left commentators argue that the Biden administration’s support of Israel and failure to stop what they call a genocide in Gaza was a major — perhaps the major — reason Harris lost in 2024, because part of the Democratic base was disillusioned and didn’t turn out. So, some on the left have suggested, if Stevens is the nominee, Democrats are risking the seat by disillusioning the same voters.
The mirror of this argument, from the establishment, blames the left for causing this disillusionment. By criticizing rival Democrats in harsh terms and casting them as pawns of special interests, they prime their voters to sit out the general election after their own candidates fail to win the nomination. “Bernie Sanders is the reason that Donald Trump is president,” famed Democratic consultant James Carville said last week, arguing this was because Sanders convinced his own supporters the Democratic Party was corrupt. 
This fight has played out in miniature in Michigan. Stevens has accused El-Sayed of undermining Democrats in 2024 by backing the “Uncommitted” movement, which sought to protest Biden’s Israel policy in the primaries. El-Sayed, conscious of this criticism, has gone out of his way to emphasize his early support for Harris in 2024, in contrast to others who sat out the race. But he’s also warned that the wrong Senate candidate could recreate the conditions that led to her loss. 
Last week, a post from one El-Sayed supporter that he would not vote for Stevens in the general election went viral. “The Democratic Party needs to be anti-AIPAC, not pro-AIPAC, and it only happens if candidates like Haley Stevens don’t win in general elections,” Adeeb Hafeez posted on X.
The next day — after critics argued this could lead to an even bigger conservative Supreme Court majority — Hafeez walked it back a bit. He posted that he “may hold my nose and end up voting for” Stevens after all in the general if she advances — but that he’d “be very uncomfortable in doing so.”
The exchange underscored the challenge for Democrats in coming together after the primary. No matter who wins, they’ll carry the scars of not only this race, but election after election pitting left-wing candidates against moderates, each of which have left behind bruised feelings and mutual suspicion.

news.title: Pakistan-origin physician Asif Mahmood elected chair of US commission on religious freedom
news.text:
Dr Asif Mahmood, a physician of Pakistani origin, has been elected as the chair of the United States Commission on International Religious Freedom (USCIRF).

The commission is a bipartisan body of ​the US federal government that monitors religious freedom around the world and makes policy recommendations ⁠to the president, the secretary of state and the US Congress.

In a statement on Monday, USCIRF announced the unanimous election of Mahmood as the panel’s chair and of CeCe Heil as the vice chair for the 2026-27 term.

Mahmood is a practising pulmonologist, human rights advocate, and philanthropist, according to USCIRF.

“His global advocacy work and humanitarian interventions have helped save lives in crisis-stricken regions around the world, including Kashmir, Tigray, and other conflict zones,” the commission’s website states.

“As a two-term member of the Medical Board of California, Dr Mahmood fiercely advocates for patients’ rights and works to secure greater access to medical coverage for all communities,” USCIRF highlighted.

In a statement after his election, posted on the USCIRF’s website, Mahmood said, “Threats to international religious freedom are occurring every day, making USCIRF’s mandate to analyse and recommend policy solutions to the US government key to advancing religious freedom abroad.“

Separately, he posted on X: “It is a remarkable journey — from a small rural village in Pakistan to leading one of the world’s foremost bodies dedicated to defending freedom of religion or belief and shaping US foreign policy in accordance with human rights globally.”

The physician affirmed his commitment to “advocate for the rights of every person: those of all faiths and those who choose no faith at all”.

“Together, let us work toward a world where freedom of religion or belief, mutual respect, human dignity, and peaceful coexistence are protected for everyone,” he added.

Mahmood was first appointed to USCIRF by House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries (D-NY) in 2024 and reappointed for a second term in 2026. He served as the commission’s vice chair during his previous term, and has been living in California since 1999, according to his website.

USCIRF, which Mahmood will now chair, had last month urged the US State Department to declare India a “country of particular concern”, citing its 2026 assessment and hearing over “ongoing, systematic, and egregious religious freedom conditions” in the country.

In 2025, the panel also highlighted that religious freedom conditions in Pakistan and India had deteriorated over the previous year. It called for sanctions against Pakistani officials and government agencies, as well as against India’s spy agency Research & Analysis Wing (RAW).

Mahmood has also contributed to efforts to garner support in the US for incarcerated former prime minister Imran Khan, particularly during the previous Biden administration.

As part of these efforts, PTI supporters have arranged seminars and public meetings and hosted numerous fundraisers for congressional leaders and senators, with one such event in 2024 co-hosted by Mahmood for then-US vice president Kamala Harris.

Additional input from Reuters

news.title: On This Day, July 21: Monkey Trial ends with guilty verdict in Tennessee
news.text:
July 21 (UPI) -- On this date in history:

In 1861, the first major military engagement of the Civil War occurred at Bull Run Creek, Va.

In 1918, a German U-boat fired on the town of Orleans, Mass., on Cape Cod peninsula, damaging a tug boat and sinking four barges, and severely injuring one man. It was the only place in the United States to receive an enemy attack during World War I.

In 1925, the so-called Monkey Trial, which pitted Clarence Darrow against William Jennings Bryan in Dayton, Tenn., in one of the great confrontations in legal history, ended with John Thomas Scopes convicted and fined $100 for teaching evolution in violation of state law.

In 1969, U.S. astronauts Neil Armstrong and Edwin "Buzz" Aldrin, lifted off from the moon in the Apollo 11 lunar module Eagle and docked with the command module Columbia piloted by Michael Collins.

In 1970, after 11 years of construction, the massive Aswan High Dam across the Nile River in Egypt was completed, ending the cycle of flood and drought in the Nile River region but triggering an environmental controversy.

In 2000, a report from special counsel John Danforth cleared U.S. Attorney General Janet Reno and the government of wrongdoing in the April 19, 1993, fire that ended the Branch Davidian siege near Waco, Texas.

In 2007, Harry Potter and the Deathly Hallows, the seventh and final installment in the best-selling series, sold more than 8.3 million copies on its first day in bookstores.

In 2011, Greece continued efforts to climb out of a financial chasm with a second bailout pledge from other eurozone countries and the International Monetary Fund worth $157 billion. Earlier, the nation dealt with its debt crisis with the help of a $146 billion loan package.

In 2024, President Joe Biden dropped his re-election bid in the 2024 presidential race, formally endorsing his vice president, Kamala Harris. Former President Donald Trump defeated Harris in November 2024 to win his second term in office.

In 2025, the U.S. Olympic and Paralympic Committee announced that it plans to follow U.S. President Donald Trump's executive order banning transgender women from women's sports.

news.title: Pakistani-American elected chair of US body on religious freedom
news.text:
Pakistani-American physician and human rights advocate Dr Asif Mahmood has been unanimously elected as the Chair of the US Commission on International Religious Freedom (USCIRF), becoming the first South Asian to lead the bipartisan federal advisory body.

"I am deeply humbled and honoured to serve as Chair of the USCIRF. It is a remarkable journey-from a small rural village in Pakistan to leading one of the world’s foremost bodies dedicated to defending freedom of religion or belief and shaping US Foreign Policy in accordance with human rights globally", Dr Mahmood said in his acceptance statement.

He remarked that his "commitment is to advocate for the rights of every person: those of all faiths and those who chose no faith at all. Together, let us work toward a world where freedom of religion or belief, mutual respect, human dignity, and peaceful coexistence are protected for everyone"

Dr Mahmood previously served as the commission's vice chair. He was reappointed for a second term in May 2026, extending his tenure through May 14, 2028. He had been serving as a commissioner for more than two years.

Speaking to Geo News following his reappointment, Dr Mahmood had described the appointment as a tremendous honour, noting that he is "the first Pakistani, the first Muslim, and the first Asian American to serve consecutive terms on the Commission".

Established in 1998 under legislation signed by then-President Bill Clinton, USCIRF is an independent, bipartisan advisory commission that monitors religious freedom worldwide. Its findings and policy recommendations are submitted to the White House, the US Department of State, and Congress, making it one of the principal advisory bodies on international religious freedom issues.

The commission consists of nine commissioners appointed by the US President and senior leaders of both the Democratic and Republican parties in Congress. Dr Mahmood was reappointed by House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries.

Political observers have described Dr Mahmood's election as Chair as particularly noteworthy given his Democratic Party affiliation and his appointment during the Trump administration. He succeeds Vicky Hartzler, the former US Representative from Missouri, who previously served as the Commission's Chair.

Dr Mahmood's appointment is also expected to draw attention in India, where his previous work has generated significant debate. Last year, he played a leading role in preparing a highly critical USCIRF report documenting alleged violations of religious freedom and the treatment of religious minorities during Prime Minister Narendra Modi's tenure.

During a commission hearing in May, Dr Mahmood stated that authorities in Indian Illegally Occupied Jammu and Kashmir were collecting data on mosque imams and urged that India be designated a "Country of Particular Concern" for serious violations of religious freedom.

The commission's annual report further recommended that the Trump administration press India to allow USCIRF and the US State Department to independently assess the country's religious freedom situation. It also called for sanctions against India's intelligence agency, the Research and Analysis Wing (RAW), and the Rashtriya Swayamsevak Sangh (RSS).

Additionally, USCIRF recommended freezing the assets of officials found responsible for severe violations of religious freedom, restricting their entry into the United States, and linking US security assistance and bilateral trade policies to measurable improvements in religious freedom in India.

Dr Mahmood is widely regarded as being closely associated with several senior Democratic leaders, including members of the Clinton family and former Vice President Kamala Harris.

In 2022, he contested California's 40th Congressional District as the Democratic nominee. Although Republican Incumbent Young Kim retained the seat, Dr Mahmood secured 122,722 votes, compared with Kim's 161,589 votes.

Earlier, in 2018, Dr Mahmood ran for California Insurance Commissioner, receiving approximately 850,000 votes, according to Ballotpedia. His bid remained unsuccessful. 

However, Congressman Brad Sherman said Dr Mahmood had received more votes than any Muslim candidate in the history of US elections at the time.

A resident of Bradbury, California, Dr Mahmood is a pulmonologist by profession and is internationally recognised for his advocacy on human rights issues.

Originally from Kharian in Pakistan's Punjab province, he earned his medical degree from Sindh Medical College before moving to the United States. He completed his residency at the University of Kentucky Medical Center and later pursued a pulmonary fellowship at Harlem Hospital, affiliated with Columbia University.

Dr Mahmood also serves on one of the United States' leading medical regulatory bodies, the Medical Board of California, to which he was appointed by Governor Gavin Newsom in January 2023. His current term runs through 2027.

In recognition of his humanitarian service, the California Hospital Association, which represents more than 500 hospitals, honoured him with its Humanitarian Award two years ago.

He also serves on the boards of the Organisation for Social Media Safety and the East Los Angeles College Foundation, and has been associated with UNICEF.

Within the Pasadena community, Dr Mahmood is also known for providing free medical treatment to patients who could not afford healthcare, a commitment that has earned him widespread respect beyond his professional and public service roles.

news.title: AOC 2028? When You Think About It, She's Really All the Dems Have
news.text:
Initially, her winning the 2028 nomination would complete the socialist takeover. The traditional Democratic Party would disappear, replaced by a new, unhinged, and authoritarian party. The good news is that the more these individuals speak, the more voters they scare away. The bad news is that society has hit a rough patch if enough people are naive enough to think that failed policies that have wiped out hundreds of millions in Russia, China, and elsewhere could work here, among other issues. It’s a failure of our education system, though leftists are inherently historically illiterate. The refusal of the Left to challenge their ideas only worsens this slide into insanity.

For the most part, AOC stayed within the confines of New York, but now she’s organizing anti-oligarchy rallies with Sen. Bernie Sanders (I-VT), increasing her visibility through endorsements, and taking all the necessary steps to grow her profile. She has a national following, can raise funds, and energizes her base—she might win the nomination. I’m not counting her out; no one should. Vice President JD Vance knows this, partly because the Democrats have no strong bench. AOC and maybe Kamala Harris, as terrible as she is, are the only two who could mount a serious campaign. Pete Buttigieg is a no-go, Bernie Sanders is too old, Sen. Cory Booker (D-NJ) is too regional, and most of the bench was exposed as a mile wide and an inch deep during the 2020 primaries.

Ocasio-Cortez was mocked as a bartender by our side, which I saw as a mistake. That’s the point, fellas: you could be a bartender today, and a member of Congress tomorrow. That’s America. It’s a good story, too. Sorry, but it resonates, which Joe Crowley found out the hard way.

She’s being smart, likely seeing how these Democratic Socialists of America candidates do in the midterms. If it’s not a slaughter, AOC watch is on hardcore. If they get throttled, there’s always another office she could occupy in her journey to the White House: it’s the one occupied by Sen. Chuck Schumer (D-NY) presently. AOC is young; we’ll need to track her for the next generation or so, but congresswoman for her entire career in public life? Please. If the White House isn’t in the cards next cycle, Chuck Schumer or the governor’s mansion could be next. And then from there, who knows? 

What’s also true is that, like the far left, there’s always whining, and she’s no exception, namely that some of the foot soldiers of the left don’t show up to help her. And that, maybe, 2028 might not be mature enough for the party's progressive left. Still, it circles back to who, on the Democrats' side, can win or has a shot at winning?

Not Gov. Josh Shapiro (D-PA). It's sad to say this, but his Jewish roots disqualify him from running as a Democrat.

Gov. Wes Moore (D-MD)? No one knows who he is.

Kamala Harris? Sure, she might be more popular with Democrats than polls suggest, but she's a perpetual loser, unqualified, and afraid to make tough decisions.

Gov. Gavin Newsom's (D-CA) stock is over. 

What a circus.
news.author: Matt Vespa

news.title: Dilip Kumar To Shah Rukh Khan: Indians Who Refused Iconic Hollywood Films
news.text: From Fahadh Faasil and Dilip Kumar to Sridevi and Shah Rukh Khan, a look at Indian actors who reportedly came close to starring in iconic Hollywood blockbusters but ultimately missed out.IMAGE: Fahadh Faasil in Aavesham; Tom Cruise and Riz Ahmed in Digger.Key PointsFahadh Faasil and Vir Das were considered for a role in Alejandro G Iñárritus Digger, which eventually went to Riz Ahmed.Dilip Kumar famously turned down the role of Sherif Ali in Lawrence of Arabia, a decision he later regretted.Sridevi confirmed being approached for a role in Steven Spielbergs Jurassic Park, but declined as crossing over to Hollywood felt alien for Indian actors at the time.After the trailer of Alejandro G Iñárritus new film Digger came out, more than Tom Cruises startling transformation as the ageing billionaire in the film, Indian fans were discussing Riz Ahmeds presence in it.The reason?It is widely reported that Fahadh Faasil was earlier considered for the role. The actor himself confirmed this in an interview with Cue Studio, where he revealed that he was approached for a role in an Alejandro G Iñárritu film but didnt go ahead with it because the makers wanted him to undergo three months of unpaid accent training. It is because of this interview that fans believe Fahadh Faasil was approached for Riz Ahmeds part.The speculation gains further traction because Ahmeds look in the trailer closely resembles the Malayalam actors real-life appearance.Fahadh Faasil was not the only Indian actor known to have been in the running for that Indian character, which ironically ended up going to a British actor of Pakistani origin. Vir Das also confirmed that he had auditioned for the role twice.It is definitely a loss for Indian fans not to see either of these two sharing screen space with Tom Cruise. Vir Das, meanwhile, hasnt been entirely unlucky in Hollywood, having appeared in films and shows like The Bubble, Whiskey Cavalier and Fresh Off The Boat.There have been successful Indian exports to Hollywood like the late Om Puri, Saeed Jaffrey, Irrfan Khan and, of course, more recently, Priyanka Chopra. But there have also been missed opportunities, either because of scheduling conflicts or disagreements over the content, with these films eventually moving ahead with different actors.Of course, we dont know whether these rejections happened during the audition stage or after the actors were shortlisted, but they certainly create a fascinating sense of what if.Sreeju Sudhakaran looks back at famous Indian actors who almost became part of iconic Hollywood films. Dilip Kumar: Lawrence Of Arabia (1962)IMAGE: Dilip Kumar in Mughal-E-Azam; Omar Sharif in Lawrence Of Arabia.David Leans 1962 classic featured Peter OToole in the title role while Omar Sharif played the important supporting character Sherif Ali ibn el Kharish, earning an Oscar nomination for his performance.But before Sharif landed the now iconic role, complete with one of cinemas greatest introductions, David Lean reportedly wanted to cast Dilip Kumar after being impressed by his performances in Hindi films he had watched while married to Leela Matkar from Girgaum, south Bombay (his longest marriage from 1960 to 1978).Dilip Kumar is said to have turned down the role because of the lengthy shooting schedule and because he didnt particularly like the directors tone.Years later, Javed Akhtar recalled asking Dilip Kumar whether he regretted rejecting any films. The legendary actor reportedly named three: Pyaasa, Baiju Bawra and Lawrence Of Arabia. Guess missing out on that Oscar nomination did sting.Sridevi: Jurassic Park (1993)IMAGE: Sridevi in Mr India; Jurassic Park.In an interview with Bollywood Hungama while promoting her 2017 movie Mom, the late Sridevi had confirmed that she had been approached for a role in Steven Spielbergs sci-fi masterpiece Jurassic Park.She turned it down because, at the time, crossing over to Hollywood felt alien for Indian actors, though she admitted it is now considered a matter of prestige.Some, however, believe she was actually approached for its sequel, The Lost World: Jurassic Park (1997). The exact role she was considered for has never been revealed.Govinda: Avatar (2009)IMAGE: Govinda in Partner; Zoe Saldana and Sam Worthington in Avatar.Take this story however you like, but Govinda has repeatedly claimed on camera that he was approached to play the lead role in James Camerons Avatar, way back in the 90s.The same role eventually went to Sam Worthington, who played Jake Sully.The fun doesnt end there.Although Govinda admitted he never met James Cameron, he claimed he was the one who suggested the title Avatar to those who approached him with the project.While the story has been met with widespread scepticism, the actor has continued to stand by it on various platforms, including in his appearance on Mukesh Khannas podcast.Shah Rukh Khan: Slumdog Millionaire (2008)IMAGE: Shah Rukh Khan in Pathaan; Anil Kapoor in Slumdog Millionaire.Around the time Danny Boyles Oscar-winning Slumdog Millionaire was being shot, Shah Rukh Khan had taken over as the host of Kaun Banega Crorepati for its third season, briefly replacing Amitabh Bachchan.So it made perfect sense for the makers to approach him to play the host of a similar quiz show in the film. It also made perfect sense that SRK rejected it.Speaking at the World Government Summit 2024 in Dubai, the superstar said he declined the role because the character was cheating and being dishonest as a host.Considering he was hosting the real show at the time, it definitely wasnt the image he wanted to project. Anil Kapoor famously stepped into the role, and the rest, as they say, is history.Aishwarya Rai Bachchan: Troy (2004)IMAGE: Aishwarya Rai in The Pink Panther 2; Brian Cox and Rose Byrne in Troy.Well, The Odyssey has just arrived, so its a good time to revisit the story of another Homer adaptation that almost featured one of Indias biggest stars.At the peak of her career in the 2000s, Aishwarya Rai was actively exploring Hollywood opportunities. She was reportedly offered a role in Wolfgang Petersens Troy, though not as Helen of Troy (played by Diane Kruger).Instead, Ash was considered for Briseis, Achilles captive and love interest. Aishwarya declined the role because she was uncomfortable with the lengthy shooting schedule and the nude and intimate scenes opposite Brad Pitt, something she later confirmed herself.Rose Byrne eventually played the character. Aishwarya, later, appeared in international projects like Bride And Prejudice, The Mistress Of Spices, The Last Legion and The Pink Panther 2.Lara Dutta: The Matrix Sequels (2003)IMAGE: Lara Dutta in Bhagam Bhag; Monica Bellucci in The Matrix Reloaded.From one former beauty queen to another. Even before making her Bollywood debut, the former Miss Universe was reportedly offered a role in one of Hollywoods biggest franchises.In a 2010 interview with Bollywood Hungama, Lara Dutta confirmed she had been approached for The Matrix franchise in 2001, which means the offer was likely for either The Matrix Reloaded (2003) or The Matrix Revolutions (2003).She turned it down because her mother was unwell. Dutta later made her Bollywood debut opposite Akshay Kumar in Andaz, which also came out in 2003.While it has never been confirmed which role she was offered, rumours suggest it was either Persephone, eventually played by Monica Bellucci, or Kamala, Satis mother, played by Tharini Mudaliar.Interestingly, Priyanka Chopra, who won Miss World the same year Dutta won Miss Universe and later co-starred with her in Andaz, played Sati in the 2021 legacy sequel, The Matrix Resurrections.Naseeruddin Shah: Harry Potter And The Prisoner Of Azkaban (2004)IMAGE: Naseeruddin Shah in Main Vaapas Aaunga; Michael Gambon in Harry Potter And The Prisoner Of Azkaban.Naseeruddin Shah has appeared in his fair share of English-language films, most notably as Captain Nemo in The League Of Extraordinary Gentlemen (2003).But he also had the opportunity to audition for one of cinemas biggest franchises. Following Richard Harris death after Harry Potter And The Chamber Of Secrets (2002), the studio was searching for someone to replace him as Hogwarts headmaster Albus Dumbledore.Naseeruddin Shah later revealed that he had been invited to audition but chose not to. While it would have been wonderful to see the actor in such an iconic role, we doubt he would have landed it anyway, considering J K Rowling was famously insistent on British actors being cast in the series.Michael Gambon eventually took over the role, beginning with Harry Potter And The Prisoner Of Azkaban.Irrfan Khan: The Martian (2015)IMAGE: Irrfan Khan in Jurassic World; Kristen Wiig and Chiwetel Ejiofor in The Martian.The late Irrfan Khan remains one of Indias most successful exports to Hollywood, having appeared in franchises like The Amazing Spider-Man, Jurassic World and The Da Vinci Code series, while also impressing in standalone films like The Warrior, A Mighty Heart, Slumdog Millionaire, The Namesake and Life of Pi.Yet, there were some famous roles he turned down, one of them being Ridley Scotts The Martian.In an interview with Mumbai Mirror, Irrfan revealed he had to pass on the film because he had already committed his dates to Shoojit Sircars Piku.Though he never explicitly confirmed the role, it is widely believed he was considered for NASA director Vincent Kapoor. Despite the character being of Indian origin, the role eventually went to English actor Chiwetel Ejiofor.Irrfan Khan was also offered a role in Christopher Nolans Interstellar but had to decline because of prior commitments to The Lunchbox and D-Day. While the late legend did have some bad luck with his Hollywood career, we can hardly complain because we got some terrific performances back home from him.Deepika Padukone: Furious 7 (2015)IMAGE: Vin Diesel and Deepika Padukone in xXx: Return of Xander Cage; Nathalie Emmanuel in Furious 7.Deepika Padukone made her Hollywood debut opposite Vin Diesel in xXx: Return of Xander Cage (2017). But the two could have shared screen space even earlier.The actress was reportedly offered a role in Furious 7, believed to be that of the hacker Ramsey, who was ultimately played by Nathalie Emmanuel.However, Deepika had already committed her dates to Sanjay Leela Bhansalis Goliyon Ki Raasleela Ram-Leela (2013) and had to pass on the opportunity.While Furious 7 went on to become a global blockbuster, its hard to imagine Deepika having many regrets. She eventually got her Hollywood break anyway, Goliyon Ki Raasleela Ram-Leela was a huge hit and working on that film also gave her something far more valuable: Future husband, Ranveer Singh.Of course, Furious 7 did star another Bollywood actor: Ali Fazal.Ronit Roy: Zero Dark Thirty (2012)IMAGE: Rishi Kapoor and Ronit Roy in Student Of The Year; Chris Pratt in Zero Dark Thirty.During an appearance on The Kapil Sharma Show while promoting Shehzada, Ronit Roy revealed one of the biggest regrets of his career: Missing out on Kathryn Bigelows Oscar-winning Zero Dark Thirty.While he didnt specify which role he had been offered, Roy said he had to turn it down because the dates clashed with his schedule for Student Of The Year. Despite requesting a change, the production refused to release him from those dates, forcing him to let go of what could have been his Hollywood debut.The real tragedy? The Student of the Year schedule didnt go ahead as planned, which meant he could have done Zero Dark Thirty after all. What a bummer!Photographs curated by Satish Bodas/Rediff

news.title: The Morning Briefing: Pass the Prilosec — Kamala Harris Is Getting Mentioned a Lot
news.text:
Top O' the Briefing

Happy Tuesday, dear Kruiser Morning Briefing friends. Please leave your suggestions for the koi pond repurposing with Margarethe in Men's Fragrances. 

The world that the Democrats occupy is different than anything I've experienced here in reality, and I'm sure all of you feel the same. It often seems as if I am staring into a giant aquarium of dysfunctionality, looking at an environment in which I know I couldn't survive, even if I wanted to, which I don't. 

Watching the Dems' abject mediocrities repeatedly fail upward never fails to perplex me, even though I've been seeing it for decades. We don't have any experience with that kind of backwards lunacy on our side of the aisle, so it will always be weird. 

Former Vice President Kamala Harris is the most notable example of this in the Democratic Party. After being so unpopular in the 2020 Democratic presidential primary season that she was out of the race in 2019, she ended up being offered the Number Two spot on the ticket. That, of course, happened because the Democrats prioritze pandering and diversity above all else. 

Which is why, despite her spectacular defeat as the 2024 anointee, Princess Cackles is still hanging around. 

Harris was unable to get any traction whatsoever in the California gubernatorial race, which she took as a sign that she should run for president again. The delusion is strong with this one. Sure, that can be said about any Democrat these days, but ex-Madame Veep really stands out in this regard. 

We have discussed this a few times in recent months. It's been pretty obvious that she's gunning for another run in 2028, but, up to this point, it has seemed as if the only interest in her doing so was confined to her fever dream. That seems to be changing a bit. 

This was shared in a post by my Twitchy colleague justmindy:

Make no mistake: Kamala Harris is very much looking like a presidential contender–in–waiting.“She’s been listening to the American people and really trying to figure out what’s impacting them and how she can be of service to them,” said Minyon Moore, a longtime Democratic… pic.twitter.com/49oGzV0z8z— New York Magazine (@NYMag) July 20, 2026

After the contortions that the Democrats' money people and their flying monkeys in the mainstream media put themselves through in order to attempt to drag Harris across the finish line in 2024, they can probably fall back into line for her in a hurry. 

The Democratic Party is in such disarray right now that they are looking at options for the next presidential race that are all equally awful. That obviously works in Harris's favor. The same thing that thrust her into prominence in 2020 is her biggest advantage. This is from a column that Daniel McCarthy wrote for us yesterday:

Harris checks the identity-politics boxes that may be as important as winning to Democratic primary voters: Their ticket must have a woman, and it can't be all white.

There is still a lot of chatter about California Governor Gavin Newsom, but I've said for years that he's a bit too white for the Democratic panderfest. Also, as we have seen so often, the Democrats have a penchant for doubling-down on their bad ideas, and Harris is certainly one of their worst. 

It's important to remember that the Democrats feel that their diversity shtick entitles them to things, like the presidency. They don't really care about minorities, as we know, they see them as reliable voting blocs and virtue-signaling currency. They no doubt feel that Kamala Harris is owed some quality time in the White House. 

And, as Daniel notes in his column, Harris could end up being the least commie option for the Dems, should they choose to back away from that.

I don't think that they want to be any less commie now that it's all out in the open. If they choose not to do a head-fake away from the precipitous left, look for the principle-free Harris to lean into the Fidel Castro ideals of the Democratic Socialists. 

Kamala Harris isn't going anywhere, we know that now. She's also not going to beat JD Vance or Marco Rubio in a presidential election.

So there's that.

news.text:
Used to go tabbing almost every night, clickin' 'til I thought I'd lose my breath ...

Told you this was going to be a problem for Talarico. https://t.co/mQthXHeb3U pic.twitter.com/aHs0iOna7p— Bonchie (@bonchieredstate) July 20, 2026

Ed: I have warned about the same problem, repeatedly. Maybe "warned" isn't the best word here; I'm down with the Jasmine Crockett struggle in this case. All kidding aside, though, all of the toxicity of "intersectionality" will emerge here in Texas, but it will expand into every Democrat jurisdiction in upcoming cycles.

WSJ: Rowling, who had contributed more than $750,000 to groups supporting Cornyn, is one of the high-dollar Republican donors who could be key to Paxton’s efforts in a competitive race. But Rowling, a onetime supporter of Paxton, had been turned off by his alleged extramarital affairs and was emotional over his drubbing of Cornyn, he said. He initially declined to help.

Then Rowling got a call from Sen. Ted Cruz, who asked him to meet with Paxton. Rowling said he had begun to consider whether Democratic opponent James Talarico was a concerning enough threat for him to open his wallet again.

He ended up taking a meeting with Paxton and Cruz to hear them out.
